source: Kāore i te whakaritea he ikiiki tāpiri, ā, kāore ngā tereina o-runga e tau ki Wembley, ā, ko ngā wāhi whakatū waka, ā, kāore i te wātea ki te papa ngā whakaritenga whakatū waka me te eke.
reference: No extra transport is being put on and overground trains will not stop at Wembley, and car parking and park-and-ride facilities are unavailable at the ground.

source: Nā ngā mataku o te korenga o te ikiiki i hīkina te whakaaro me tākaro pea te kēmu ki mua i ngā kūaha kati horekau ngā kaitautoko o te kapa.
reference: Fears of lack of transportation raised the possibility that the game would be forced to play behind closed doors without the team's supporters.

source: I rīpoata tētahi rangahau i whakaputahia i te taite i te puka pūtaiao mō ngā āhuatanga o tētahi manu hou i te motu Ecuador Galapagos.
reference: A study published on Thursday in the journal Science reported on formation of a new bird species on the Ecuadorean Galápagos Islands.

source: I pūrongotia e ngā kairangahau i Princeton University ki Ngā Whenua Tōpū o Amerika, i Uppsala University ki Huitene e rua tau noa iho te roa o te kukuwhatanga, ahakoa i whakaponotia ka roa rawa ake tēnei hātepe, i runga i te whakatipu i waenganui i tētahi Darwin finch taketake, a Geospiza fortes, me te cactus finch tauhou, a Geospiza conirostris.
reference: Researchers from Princeton University in the United States and Uppsala University in Sweden reported the new species evolved in just two generations, though this process had been believed to take much longer, due to breeding between an endemic Darwin finch, Geospiza fortes, and the immigrant cactus finch, Geospiza conirostris.

source: Ka hangaia pea te koura ki ngā tini momo āhua. Ka taea te takapapa ki ngā āhua ririki.
reference: Gold may be worked into all sorts of shapes. It can be rolled into tiny shapes.

source: Ka taea te kukume ki roto i te waea angiangi, mā reira ka taea te takawiri me te whiri. E taea te hamahama, te rōra rānei kia hītingia.
reference: It can be pulled into thin wire, which can be twisted and plaited. It can be hammered or rolled into sheets.

source: Ētahi wā ka tīno ririki, me te tau ki mētara kē atu. E taea ana te hanga kia ririki rawa e āhei ai ētahi wā te whakarākei i ngā pikitia kua tā-āringa ki roto i ngā pukapuka kua ingoatia "illuminated manuscripts".
reference: It can be made very thin, and stuck onto other metal. It can be made so thin that it was sometimes used to decorate the hand-painted pictures in books called "illuminated manuscripts".

source: E kīia ana tēnei ko te pH o tētahi matū. Ka taea te hanga i tētahi tūtohu ki te wairanu a te kāpeti whero.
reference: This is called a chemical's pH. You can make an indicator using red cabbage juice.

source: Ka huri te tae o te wai kāpeti i runga i tōna waikawa, i runga rānei i te kawakore o te matū.
reference: The cabbage juice changes color depending on how acidic or basic (alkaline) the chemical is.

source: Ka tūtohu te taumata o te pH mā te nui o te katote Hauwai (te H o te pH) o roto i te matū kua mātaitia.
reference: The pH level is indicated by the amount of Hydrogen (the H in pH) ions in the tested chemical.

source: Ko ngā katote hauwai i turaki iraoho me ngā irahiko (mai i te ngota hauwai kotahi te iraoho me te irahiko.
reference: Hydrogen ions are protons that had their electrons stripped off them (since Hydrogen atoms consist of one proton and one electron).

source: Hurihia ngā paura maroke e rua, ā, mā ō ringaringa, mahia ki tētahi pōro.
reference: Swirl the two dry powders together and then, with clean wet hands, squeeze them into a ball.

source: Ko ngā tōmairangi o ōu ringa ka tauhohe me ngā wāhi kiritai o waho, ā, ka hanga katakata nei te rongo, ka hanga hoki i tētahi momo anga.
reference: The moisture on your hands will react with the outer layers, which will feel funny and form a sort of shell.

source: Kei ngā tāone nui o Harappa, o Mohenjo-daro hoki tētahi wharepaku wai ki te tata ki ngā whare katoa, e tūhonotia ana ki tētahi pūnaha paru pīroiroi.
reference: The cities of Harappa and Mohenjo-daro had a flush toilet in almost every house, attached to a sophisticated sewage system.

source: Kua kitea ngā toenga o ngā pūnaha paru ki ngā whare o ngā tāone nui Minoan o Crete me Santorini ki Kariki.
reference: Remains of sewage systems have been found in the houses of the Minoan cities of Crete and Santorini in Greece.

source: He wharepaku hoki i neherā ki Īhipa, ki Pāhia, ki Haina anō hoki. Ki te nōhanga Rōmana, he wāhanga ngā wharepaku i ētahi wā o ngā whare kaukau tūmatanui i reira ngā tāne rātou ko ngā wāhine i te noho ngātahi.
reference: There were also toilets in ancient Egypt, Persia and China. In Roman civilization, toilets were sometimes part of public bath houses where men and women were together in mixed company.

source: Ki te karangatia e koe tētahi he mano mairo te tawhiti, kei te whakamahia e koe he amiorangi.
reference: When you call someone who is thousands of miles away, you are using a satellite.

source: Ka karangahia te āmiorangi, ka tukua wawetia mai te karanga ki raro.
reference: The satellite in space gets the call and then reflects it back down, almost instantly.

source: I tukuna te āmiorangi ki te tuarangi e tētahi rākete. Kei te whakamahia ngā karu whātata ki tuarangi e ngā kaipūtaiao nā te mea kei te rāweke te kōhauhau i ētahi o tā mātou aho, tirohanga anō hoki.
reference: The satellite was sent into space by a rocket. Scientists use telescopes in space because the Earth’s atmosphere distorts some of our light and view.

source: Mā te rākete rahi nui ake i te 100 putu te teitei e tuku i tētahi āmiorangi, karu whātata ranei ki tuarangi.
reference: It takes a giant rocket over a 100 feet high to put a satellite or telescope in space.

source: Kua tīno tīni te ao nei nā te wīra. Ko te mea nui a te wīra he haerenga tere ake, he haerenga māmā ake hoki.
reference: The wheel has changed the world in incredible ways. The biggest thing that the wheel has done for us is given us much easier and faster transportation.

source: Nāna i tō mai i te tereina, i te waka, i ētahi atu momo waka ikiiki ki a tātou.
reference: It has brought us the train, the car, and many other transportation devices.

source: Kei raro i ērā ko ētahi ngeru he rahi waenga ka kai i ngā kararehe he rahi te waenga atu i te rāpiti ki te anaterope ki te tia.
reference: Under them are more medium sized cats that eat medium sized prey ranging from rabbits to antelopes and deer.

source: Whainari, he tini ngā momo ngeru iti (me ngā ngeru mōkai āwhiowhio hoki) ka kai i ngā tini mea iti nei pēnei i ngā ngārara, i ngā paihamu, i ngā mokomoko me ngā manu.
reference: Finally, there are many small cats (including loose pet cats) that eat the far more numerous small prey like insects, rodents, lizards, and birds.

source: Ko te take i angitu ai rātou ko te ariā o te tūranga ake, arā, ko tētahi tūranga motuhake kei ia ngeru kia kaua ia e tatawhāinga ki ērā atu.
reference: The secret to their success is the concept of the niche, a special job each cat holds that keeps it from competing with others.

source: Ko ngā raiona ngā ngeru tīno whakahoahoa, e noho ana ki ngā rōpū e kīia ana ko te pride.
reference: Lions are the most social cats, living in large groups called prides.

source: Ki roto i tētahi paraite i waenganui i te kotahi ki te toru ngā taurawhi pakeke he whanaunga rātou, tahi ki te tae atu ki te toru tekau uwha me ngā punua.
reference: Prides are made up of one to three related adult males, along with as many as thirty females and cubs.

source: He pānga kiritahi te nuinga o ngā wāhine tētahi ki tētahi i te nuinga o te wā, nā te mea he whānau nui o ngā tuakana/teina me ngā tamāhine.
reference: The females are usually closely related to each other, being a large family of sisters and daughters.

source: Ka āhua pērā te mahi a ngā rōpū raiona i ngā rōpū wuruhi, kurī rānei, he kararehe he ohorere tana ōrite ki ngā raiona (engari kaua ki ērā atu ngeru nui) mō te whanonga, ā, he tino whakamate ki ngā kararehe ka patua e rātou.
reference: Lion prides act much like packs of wolves or dogs, animals surprisingly similar to lions (but not other big cats) in behavior, and also very deadly to their prey.

source: He kaiaka e mōhio nei ki ngā mea huhua, e taea nei e te taika te piki (heoi he āhua ninipa), te kaukau, te peke tawhiti me te kukume e rima ngā taima kaha ake i te tangata kaha.
reference: A well rounded athlete, the tiger can climb (though not well), swim, leap great distances and pull with five times the force of a strong human.

source: Kei te roopu ōrite ki te raiona, ki te rēpara me ngā hākua te tāika. Ko ēnei ngeru tokowhā anake ngā ngeru e āhei ana te horu.
reference: The tiger is in the same group (Genus Panthera) as lions, leopards, and jaguars. These four cats are the only ones who can roar.

source: Ko te haruru a te taika ehara i te mea he haruru nui pērā ki te raiona, engari he momo ngengere, he momo tīwahawaha.
reference: The tiger's roar is not like the full-voiced roar of a lion, but more like a sentence of snarly, shouted words.

source: Pai ki ngā ocelots te kai i ngā kararehe iti. Ka hopu i a rātou ngā makimaki, ngā nākahi, ngā rīroi me ngā manu mēnā e taea ana. Ko te nuinga o ngā kararehe e aru nei ngā ocelot he iti rawa iho i a ia.
reference: Ocelots like to eat small animals. They will catch monkeys, snakes, rodents and birds if they can. Almost all of the animals that the ocelot hunts are far smaller than it is.

source: E whakaaro ana ngā kaimātai pūtaiao mā te hongi e rapu ai te ocelot i āna kai, e whai ai ia ki hea rā i te whenua ōna kai e haere ana.
reference: Scientists think that ocelots follow and find animals to eat (prey) by smell, sniffing for where they've been on the ground.

source: He rawe ō rātou karu ki te titiro i roto i te pō uriuri me ngā tirohanga o te pō, me te āta nekeneke hoki. Ka aru te Ocelot i ana kai ,ā te whakaranu me ōna taiao, ā, kua kotahi atu ki tana kai.
reference: They can see very well in the dark with night vision, and move very stealthily, too. Ocelots hunt their prey by blending in with their surroundings then pouncing on their prey.

source: I te wā e wehe ai tētahi roopū iti o ētahi mea ora (he rahinga iti) i tētahi rahinga nui i ahu mai ai rātou (pēnā me i neke ake i ētahi pae māunga i tētahi awa rānei, me i nuku rānei ki tētahi motu e uaua ai te hokinga mai) i te nuinga o te wā ka tau ki tētahi taiao rerekē ki tā rātou i mōhio ai.
reference: When a small group of living things (a small population) gets separated from the main population that they came from (like if they move over a mountain range or a river, or if they move to a new island so that they can't easily move back) they will often find themselves in a different environment than they were in before.

source: Kei tēnei taiao hou he rauemi rerekē, he kaiwhakataetae rerekē, nō reira me whai te taupori hou i ngā āhuatanga rerekē, i ngā urutaunga rerekē rānei kia noho hei kaiwhakataetae kaha i ērā i te hiahiatia i mua.
reference: This new environment has different resources and different competitors, so the new population will need different features or adaptations to be a strong competitor than what they had needed before.

source: Kāore anō te taupori tāngata kia paku tīni, me whai tonu i ngā urutaunga o mua.
reference: The original population hasn't changed at all, they still need the same adaptations as before.

source: Nāwai rā, i te tīmatanga o te taupori te urutau ki tō rātou taupori hou, ka iti haere tō rātou āhua i tērā atu taupori.
reference: Over time, as the new population begins to adapt to their new environment, they start to look less and less like the other population.

source: Tāria te wā, ā muri i ngā tini mano tau, tini miriona tau rānei pea, ka pērā te rerekē o te āhua o ngā taupori e rua e kore e taea te kīia kotahi tonu te momo.
reference: Eventually, after thousands or even millions of years, the two populations will look so different that they can't be called the same species.

source: Ka karangahia tēnei āhuatanga koiora, e kī ana ko te tikanga noa ko te hanganga o tētahi koiora hou. Kāore e taea te aha mō te taha ki te koiora, ā, he wāhanga tīno hirahira tēnei o te kukuwhatanga.
reference: We call this process speciation, which just means the formation of new species. Speciation is an unavoidable consequence and a very important part of evolution.

source: Ka hangaia e te tipu te hāora ka hāngia e te tangata, ā, ka hā ki roto te tipu i te hauhā ka puta i te tangata (arā, ka hā ki waho).
reference: Plants make oxygen which humans breathe, and they take in carbon-dioxide which humans exhale (that is, breathe out).

source: Kei te hangaia e ngā tipu ā rātou ake kai mai i te rā ki te ahotakakame. Kei te tuku hoki rātou i te marumaru.
reference: Plants make their food from the sun by photosynthesis. They also provide shade.

source: Ka hangaia ō mātou whare me ngā kākahu i ngā hua tipu. Ko te nuinga o ā māua/mātou kai he hua tipu. Ki te kore ngā hua tipu e kore ngā kararehe e ora.
reference: We make our houses from plants and make clothes from plants. Most foods that we eat are plants. Without plants, animals could not survive.

source: Ko Mosasaurus te konihi matua o tōna wā, nō reira kāore i mataku ki tētahi, atu i ērā atu mosasaurs.
reference: Mosasaurus was the apex predator of its time, so it feared nothing, except other mosasaurs.

source: Kei ōna kauae roa te nui atu i te 70 ngā niho tino koi, tae atu ki tētahi huinga niho anō ki te ngao, nō reira e kore e taea te pahiko e tētahi mea ka tutaki ki a ia.
reference: Its long jaws were studded with more than 70 razor-sharp teeth, along with an extra set in the roof of its mouth, meaning that there was no escape for anything that crossed its path.

source: Kāore mātou i te tino mōhio, heoi anō, kei kīa he arero marara tōna. Ko ōna kai he honu, he ika nui, he mokoweri, he kaikiko tonu hoki.
reference: We don't know for sure, but it may have had a forked tongue. Its diet included turtles, large fish, other mosasaurs, and it may even have been a cannibal.

source: I tuki hoki ia i ngā mea katoa ka kuhu ki te wai; kāore hoki tētahi mokonui rahi pērā i te T. rex i te āhei ki te whawhai ki a ia.
reference: It also attacked anything that entered the water; even a giant dinosaur such as T. rex would be no match for it.

source: Ahakoa ka mōhio tātou ki te nuinga o ā rātou kai, i ngā Rōmana anō hoki ētahi kai hākari rerekē, whanokē, tae atu ki te poaka puihi, te pīkake, ngā ngata, me tētahi momo kiore e kīia nei he dormouse
reference: While most of their food would be familiar to us, Romans did have their share of strange or unusual feast items, including wild boar, peacock, snails, and a type of rodent called a dormouse

source: Ko tētahi atu rerekētanga ko tā te hunga pohara, te wahine anō hoki kai i ā rātou kai i te wā e noho ana ki ngā tūru, ko tā te tāne whai rawa he hākari tahi i a rātou e takoto ana ki ō rātou taha i a rātou e kai ana i ā rātou kai.
reference: Another difference was that while the poor people and the woman ate their food while sitting in chairs, the rich men liked to have banquets together where they would lounge on their sides while they ate their meals.

source: Kāore i taea e ngā kai a ngā Rōmana Tawhito te whai i ngā kai i tae atu ki Ūropi i Amerika, i Āhia rānei i ngā rau tau i muri ake.
reference: Ancient Roman meals couldn't have included foods that came to Europe from America or from Asia in later centuries.

source: Hei tauira, karekau ā rātou kānga, tōmato, rīwai, kōkō rānei, ā, kāore te Rōma o mua i kai i te tākei.
reference: For instance, they didn't have corn, nor tomatoes, nor potatoes, nor cocoa, and no ancient Roman ever tasted a turkey.

source: I hangaia e ngā Babylon he temepara mō ia o ō rātou atua, ā, i whakapono koinaka tonu te kāinga o te atua raka.
reference: The Babylonians built each of their gods a primary temple that was considered the home of the god.

source: I te kawe mai ngā tāngata i ngā whakahere ki ngā atua, ā, i ngana ngā pirihi ki te aro atu ki ngā hiahia o ngā atua mā ngā mahi whakanui, mā ngā hui ahurei hoki.
reference: People would bring sacrifices to the gods and the priests would try to attend to the needs of the gods through ceremonies and festivals.

source: I ia temepara tētahi ātea temepara, ā, he whakamaurutanga ki roto ka taea anake e ngā pirihi te kuhu.
reference: Each temple had an open temple courtyard and then an inner sanctuary that only the priests could enter.

source: I ētahi wā ka hangaia he pourewa motuhake he kōeko te āhua, ko te ingoa ko ngā ziggurats, kia noho hei wāhanga o ngā temepara.
reference: Sometimes special pyramid shaped towers, called ziggurats, were built to be a part of the temples.

source: He wāhi motuhake te tihi o te pourewa mō te atua.
reference: The top of the tower was special sanctuary for the god.

source: I roto i te āhuarangi mahana o te Waenga Rāwhiti, kāore i tino hirahira te whare.
reference: In the warm climate of the Middle East, the house was not so important.

source: Ko tā te oranga o te whānau Hīperu i waho kē.
reference: Most of the life of the Hebrew family happened in the open air.

source: Ko ngā wāhine i tunu i roto i te iāri; ko ngā toa he taupaepae kua tuwhera noa e anga ana ki te tiriti. Ko te kōhatu i whakamahia kia hanga whare.
reference: Women did the cooking in the yard; stores were just open counters looking into the street. Stone was used for building houses.

source: Karekau he ngahere nunui i te whenua o Canaan, nō reira i tino nui ai te utu mō te rākau.
reference: There were no large forests in the land of Canaan, so wood was extremely expensive.

source: I tūruki noa te whakatau i te whenua o Greenland. Kei ngā kōrero mō Norse e kī ana i manenetia a Erik the Red mai i Iceland i ana mahi kōhuru, ā, nōna e e haere whaka-te-uru ana, i kitea ai e ia a Greenland, ka tapaina te whenua nei ko Greenland.
reference: Greenland was settled sparsely. In the Norse sagas they say that Erik the Red was exiled from Iceland for murder, and when travelling further west, found Greenland and named it Greenland.

source: Ahakoa tāna huranga, i reira kē ngā iwi Eskimo e noho ana i taua wā.
reference: But regardless of his discovery, Eskimo tribes were already living there at the time.

source: Ahakoa he "Scandinavian" ia whenua, he nui ngā rerekētanga i waenganui i ngā tāngata, i ngā kīngi, i ngā tikanga, i ngā hītori o Tenemāka, o Huitene, o Nōwei, o Tiorangi.
reference: Though each country was 'Scandinavian', there were many differences between the people, kings, customs and history of Denmark, Sweden, Norway and Iceland.

source: Mēnā kua kite koe i te whakaaturanga National treasure, kei pōhēhē koe ka kitea he mapi tāonga ki runga i te Whakapuakanga o te Rangatiratanga.
reference: If you have watched the movie National Treasure, you may think a treasure map was written on the back of the Declaration of Independence.

source: Heoi anō, ehara tērā i te pono. Ahakoa he tuhinga ki te taha whakamuri o te puka, ehara i te mahere taonga.
reference: However, that is not true. Although there is something written on the back of the document, it is not a treasure map.

source: I tuhingia ki muri i te Whakapuakanga o te Rangatiratanga ko ngā kupu "te whakapuakanga tuatahi taketake mō te Rangatiratanga i te 4 o Hurae 1776". Ka kitea te tuhinga i raro o te tuhinga, e hurirapa ana.
reference: Written on the back of the Declaration of Independence were the words "Original Declaration of Independence dated 4th July 1776". The text appears on the bottom of the document, upside down.

source: He ahakoa kāore tētahi e mōhio pū nāwai i tuhi, e mōhio nei i te timatanga o tana oranga, te tuhinga pepa nui (ko tana nui ko te 29 3/4 inihi mā te 24 1/2 inihi) i rōrangia hei putunga.
reference: While no one knows for certain who wrote it, it is known that early in its life, the large parchment document (it measures 29¾ inches by 24½ inches) was rolled up for storage.

source: Nā, e tinga ana i tāpiritia noatia te tuhinga hei tapa.
reference: So, it is likely that the notation was added simply as a label.

source: Nā ngā tauranga D-Day me ngā pakanga i whai ake i wewete i te raki o Wīwī, engari kāore tonu te tonga i te wewete.
reference: The D-Day landings and the following battles had freed the north of France, but the south still wasn't free.

source: I whakaturengia e te "Vichy" Wīwi. He tangata Wīwi ēnei i hou te rongo me ngā Tiamani i te 1940, ā, i mahi tahi me ngā kaiwhawhai tēnā i te whawhai i a rātou.
reference: It was ruled by the "Vichy" French. These were French people who had made peace with the Germans in 1940 and worked with the invaders instead of fighting them.

source: I te rā 15 o Ākuhaga 1940, i eke ngā Allies ki te tonga o Wīwī, i kīia te ekenga ko "Operation Dragoon".
reference: On 15 August 1940, the Allies invaded southern France, the invasion was called "Operation Dragoon".

source: I roto i ngā wiki e rua noa i wetekina e ngā Amerikana me ngā hōia Wīwī Free French te tonga o Wīwī, ā, i te anga atu ki Tiamani.
reference: In just two weeks the Americans and Free French forces had liberated southern France and were turning towards Germany.

source: Ko te iwitanga he ahurea kotahi e whāia ana e tētahi rōpū motuhake nunui e noho ana e mahi tahi ana, he hapori.
reference: A civilization is a singular culture shared by a significant large group of people who live and work co-operatively, a society.

source: Kei te ahu mai te kupu civilization i te civilis no te reo Rātini, ko tōna tikanga ko te civil, e hāngai ana ki te kupu civis o Rātini, ko tōna tikanga ko te citizen, me te civitas, ko tōna tikanga ko te tāone nui, ko te takiwā tāone nui rānei, ā, ka pērā ka tautuhitia hoki te rahi o te papori.
reference: The word civilization comes from the Latin civilis, meaning civil, related to the Latin civis, meaning citizen, and civitas, meaning city or city-state, and that also somehow defines the size of the society.

source: Ka tū ngā rohe-tāone nui, kātahi ka puta mai ngā whenua. Ko tētahi āhuatanga o te ahurea whatutoto ko te tuku i te mātauranga whakawhiti atu i ētahi reanga, tētahi whakarerenga ā-ahurea e toe ana, ā, he tika te tukutuku atu.
reference: City-states are the precursors of nations. A civilizational culture implies the passing on of knowledge across several generations, a lingering cultural footprint and fair dissemination.

source: Ka maninohea noa ngā ahurea iti nei, ka ngaro ngā kōrero tuku iho e mana ai te iwi hei iwi mananui.
reference: Minor cultures often vanish without leaving relevant historic evidence and fail to be recognized as proper civilizations.

source: I te Revolutionary War, i hanga tuatahitia e ngā whenua tekau mā toru tētahi kāwanatanga ā-motu ngoikore - ā, ko te Congress tōna waehanga anake - ki raro i ngā Articles of Confederation.
reference: During the Revolutionary War, the thirteen states first formed a weak central government—with the Congress being its only component—under the Articles of Confederation.

source: Kāore i whai mana tō te kaunihera ki te whakatau tāke, nāreira, i te mea kāore he mana whakahaere motu he kaiwhakawā rānei, i whakawhirinaki atu ki te mana o te kāwanatanga kāore i pai ki te mahitahi hei whakamana i ana ture katoa.
reference: Congress lacked any power to impose taxes, and, because there was no national executive or judiciary, it relied on state authorities, who were often uncooperative, to enforce all its acts.

source: Karekau hoki tōna mana kia takahi i ngā ture tāke me ngā tāka i waenganui i ngā whenua.
reference: It also had no authority to override tax laws and tariffs between states.

source: Kei te hiahiatia e ngā Atikara te whakaaetanga a te katoa mai i ngā takiwā katoa i mua i te panoni, ā, ka pērā rawa te kore aronga o ngā takiwā ki te kāwanatanga ā-motu he nui ngā wā e matangaro ana ā rātou māngai.
reference: The Articles required unanimous consent from all the states before they could be amended and states took the central government so lightly that their representatives were often absent.

source: Ko te kapa whanapoikiri ā-motu o Itāria, tahi ki te kapa whanapoikiri ā-motu o Tiamani, ko te kapa angitu rawa tuarua ki te ao, ā, ko rātou ngā toa FIFA World Cup i te tau 2006.
reference: Italy's national football, along with German national football team is the second most successful team in the world and were the FIFA World Cup champions in 2006.

source: Kei roto i ngā tākaro rorotu ko te whanapoikiri, te pahiketepōro, te meke pōro, te fencing, te whutupōro, te eke pahikara, te hoki tio, te hoki rōra, me te reihi motokā F1.
reference: Popular sports include football, basketball, volleyball, water-polo, fencing, rugby, cycling, ice hockey, roller hockey and F1 motor racing.

source: He rorotu rawa ngā hākinakina hōtoke ki ngā takiwā Raki, ā, ka whakataetae te hunga nō Itāria ki ngā kēmu ā-ao, me ngā tauwhāinga o Ngā Taumāhekeheke o te Ao.
reference: Winter sports are most popular in the Northern regions, with Italians competing in international games and Olympic events.

source: Kei a Hapani ngā motu tata ki te 7,000 (ko te mea nui rawa ko Honshu), nā konei ko Hapani te motu tuawhitu te rahi rawa ki te ao!
reference: Japans holds nearly 7,000 islands (the biggest being Honshu), making Japan the 7th largest island in the world!

source: I te mea o ngā tini moutere o Hapani, ka kīa nuitia a Hapani i roto i te hanga takotoranga papa anō he "archipelago".
reference: Due to the cluster/group of islands Japan has, Japan is often referred to, on a geographical stance, as an "archipelago"

source: I Taiwana i tīmata i ngā wā o mua i te rautau 15 i te tuhinga a ngā hēramana Ūropi e whakawhiti ana i te motu ko Ilha Formosa, arā ko motu ātaahua.
reference: Taiwan beginning start way back in 15th century where European sailors passing by record the island’s name as Ilha Formosa, or beautiful island.

source: I te tau 1624,i te whakatū te Dutch East India Company i tētahi wāhi kāinga ki Taiwana ki te tonga mā uru, nā reira i tīmata ai tētahi hurihanga ki ngā tikanga whakaputa tōpata a ngā iwi taketake me te utu i ngā kaimahi Hainamana ki te mahi ki āna tupuranga raihi me te huka.
reference: In 1624,Dutch East India Company establishes a base in southwestern Taiwan, initiating a transformation in aboriginal grain production practices and employing Chinese laborers to work on its rice and sugar plantations.

source: I te 1638 ka whakahaerengia e ngā tauā a te whakareanga Qing te mana o Taiwan ki te uru me ngā wāhi takutai o te raki, ā, ka whakatauria ko Taiwan hei wāhanga o te Emapara Qing i te 1885.
reference: In 1683, Qing dynasty (1644-1912) forces take control of Taiwan’s western and northern coastal areas and declared Taiwan as a province of the Qing Empire in 1885.

source: I te tau 1895, i muri i te hinga ki te Pakanga Sino-Japanese Tuatahi (1894-1895), ka hainatia e te kāwanatanga Qing te Tiriti o Shimonoseki, nā korā ia i tuku i te tino rangatiratanga o Taiwana ki Hapani, ka noho rātou hei mana whakahaere i te motu tae atu ki 1945.
reference: In 1895, after defeat in the First Sino-Japanese War (1894-1895), the Qing government signs the Treaty of Shimonoseki, by which it cedes sovereignty over Taiwan to Japan, which rules the island until 1945.

source: Ko Machu Picchu ngā hanganga matua e toru, arā ko Intihuatana, ko te Temple of the Sun, ko te Room of the Three Windows.
reference: Machu Picchu consist of three main structures, namely Intihuatana, the Temple of the Sun, and the Room of the Three Windows.

source: Ko te nuinga o ngā whare nunui i ngā tauwharenga o te wāhi nei kua hangaia kia whai mārama pai ake ngā tūruhi ki te āhuatanga tūturu i mua atu.
reference: Most of the buildings on the edges of the complex have been rebuilt in order to give tourists a better idea of how they originally appeared.

source: Tae atu ki te tau 1976, kua whakahaumanutia te toru tekau ōrau o Machu Picchu, ā, kei te haere tonu aua mahi whakahaumanu tae noa mai ki tēnei wā.
reference: By 1976, thirty percent of Machu Picchu had been restored and restoration continues till today.

source: Hei tauira, ko te whakatakotoranga whakaahua ū noa rawa ki te ao ko te 35mm, koirā te rahi kiriata matua i te mutunga o te wā o te kiriata ā-kiko.
reference: For example, the most common still image photography format in the world is 35mm, which was the dominant film size at the close of the analog film era.

source: Kei te whakaputaina tonutia i tēnei wā, heoi anō ko te mea nui ko tōna ōwehenga aronga kua waahanga riro mai i ngā whakatakotoranga rongo whakaahua kāmera mamati
reference: It is still produced today, but more importantly its aspect ratio has been inherited by digital camera image sensor formats.

source: Ko te takotoranga 35mm, e rangirua ana i te mea he 36mm te whānui, te24mm te tāroa.
reference: The 35mm format is actually, somewhat confusingly, 36mm in width by 24mm in height.

source: Nō reira ko te ōwehenga anga o tēnei hōputu (te whakawehe ki te tekau mā rua kia hua ai te ōwehenga tauoti māmā rawa) e kīia ana ko te 3:2.
reference: The aspect ratio of this format (dividing by twelve to obtain the simplest whole-number ratio) is therefore said to be 3:2.

source: He maha ngā āhuatanga nei e rite nei e pā tata ana rānei ki tēnei karangatanga āwhata.
reference: Many common formats (APS family of formats, for example) are equal to or closely approximate this aspect ratio.

source: Ko te ture o ngā hautoru e tino takahia ana, e tino taunuhia ana, ko tētahi aratohu māmā e hanga ana i te hihiri i te wā e pupuri tonu ana te āhua o te nahanaha ki tētahi whakaahua.
reference: The much-abused and often-ridiculed rule of thirds is a simple guideline creating dynamism while keeping a measure of order in an image.

source: Kei te kī ia ko te wāhi pai rawa mō te kaupapa matua kei te pūtahi o ngā rārangi e whakawehe ana i te whakaahua ki ngā hautoru ā-poutū, ā-huapae anō hoki (tirohia te tauira).
reference: It states that the most effective place for the main subject is at the intersection of lines dividing the image into thirds vertically and horizontally (see example).

source: I roto i tēnei wāhanga mō te hitori o Ūropi, i arohaehaetia te Hāhi Katorika i ōna mahi wahi rawa.
reference: During this period of European history, the Catholic Church, which had become rich and powerful, came under scrutiny.

source: Neke atu i te kotahi mano tau kua whakakotahingia e te hāhi Karaitiana ngā whenua o Ūropi ahakoa te rerekē o ngā reo me ngā tikanga.
reference: For over a thousand years the Christian religion had bound European states together despite differences in language and customs. I

source: I pā tana kaha rangiwhāwhā katoa i te katoa atu i te kīngi ki te tūtūā.
reference: Its all-pervading power affected everyone from king to commoner.

source: Ko tētahi o ngā whakapono matua Karaitiana kia whakamahia te whai rawa hei whakamāuru i te taumahatanga, i te pōhara, ā, kei reira ngā pūtea o te whare karakia mō tērā take.
reference: One of the main Christian tenets is that wealth should be used to alleviate suffering and poverty and that the monetary funds of the church are there specifically for that reason.

source: Kua kitea te mana matua o te whare karakia ki Rōma mō te neke atu i te kotahi mano tau, ā, nā tēnei pīhangaiti o te mana me te pūtea i ara ake ki te ui a te tokomaha mēnā rānei kei te tūtakina tēnei whakapono.
reference: The central authority of the church had been in Rome for over a thousand years and this concentration of power and money led many to question whether this tenet was being met.

source: Whai muri tata i te huakitanga mai o te riri, ka whakarite ai a Piritania tētahi aukatinga ope moana mō Tiamani.
reference: Soon after the outbreak of hostilities, Britain initiated a naval blockade of Germany.

source: I kitea te pai o te rautaki, i aukatia ngā rawa waiwai mā te tauā, mā te iwi whānui, ahakoa i takahi tēnei aukati i te ture ā-ao e whakaaetia whānuitia ana kua nahanahatia e ētahi whakaaetanga ā-ao o ngā rautau e rua kua pahure ake.
reference: The strategy proved effective, cutting off vital military and civilian supplies, although this blockade violated generally accepted international law codified by several international agreements of the past two centuries.

source: I maina a Peretānia i ngā wai ā-ao hei aukati i te urunga atu o ngā waipuke ki ngā wāhanga nui o te moana, nā tērā i mōrearea hoki ai ngā waipuke ngū.
reference: Britain mined international waters to prevent any ships from entering entire sections of ocean, causing danger to even neutral ships.

source: I runga i te urupare whāiti ki tēnei rautaki, ko te kawatau a Tiamani ka pērā anō te urupare ki tāna mahi pakanga waka whakatakere here kore.
reference: Since there was limited response to this tactic, Germany expected a similar response to its unrestricted submarine warfare.

source: I te wā o ngā tau 1920, ko te waiaro o te nuinga o ngā kirirarau me te whenua he mohoaotanga, he maungārongo anō hoki.
reference: During the 1920s, the prevailing attitudes of most citizens and nations was that of pacifism and isolation.

source: Nō te kitenga atu i ngā mahi whakamataku o te Pakanga Tuatahi o te Ao, i ngana katoa ngā iwi ki te ārai atu i tērā momo āhuatanga.
reference: After seeing the horrors and atrocities of war during World War I, nations desired to avoid such a situation again in the future.

source: I te tau 1884, i neke a Tesla ki Ngā Whenua Tōpū o Amerika hei uru atu ki tētahi tūranga mahi ki te Edison Company ki New York City.
reference: In 1884, Tesla moved to the United States of America to accept a job with the Edison Company in New York City.

source: I tae atu ia ki Ngā Whenua Tōpū o Amerika me te 4 hēneti noa iho, he pukapuka rotarota, me tētahi reta taunaki mai i Charles Batchelor (tōna kaiwhakahaere ki tāna mahi o mua) ki a Thomas Edison.
reference: He arrived in the US with 4 cents to his name, a book of poetry, and a letter of recommendation from Charles Batchelor (his manager in his previous job) to Thomas Edison.

source: Kei Haina Tawhito tētahi tikanga ahurei o te whaakatu i ngā tini wā rerekē; ko ia wāhanga o Haina, ko ia whānau i whai mana rānei ko tētahi kāwai motuhake.
reference: Ancient China had a unique way of showing different time periods; each stage of China or each family that was in power was a distinctive dynasty.

source: Waihoki i waenganui i ia arikitanga ko tētahi wā tikoki o ngā takiwā e noho wehe ana. Ko te wā e mōhiotia whānuitia ana o ēnei wā ko te wā Three Kingdoms i tū mō te 60 tau te roa i waenganui i te Han me te Jin Dynasty.
reference: Also between each dynasty was an unstable age of divided provinces. The best-known of these periods was the Three Kingdoms epoch taking place for 60 years between the Han and the Jin Dynasty.

source: I ēnei wā ka tū te pakanga aungarea i waenganui i ngā tini ariki e tohe ana mō te torōna.
reference: During these periods fierce warfare took place between many nobles fighting for the throne.

source: Ko te Three Kingdoms tētahi o ngā wā tino rere te toto i te hītori o Ancient China i mate ngā tini mano tāngata e whawhai ana kia noho ki te tūru teitei rawa ki te whare kīngi whakahira ki Xi'an.
reference: The Three Kingdoms was one of the bloodiest eras in Ancient China’s history thousands of people died fighting to sit in the highest seat in the grand palace at Xi’an.

source: He nui ngā pānga ā-pāpori, ā-tōrangapū hoki pērā ki te whakamahinga o te pūnaha ine hou, ko te nekehanga ake mai i te whai tūturutanga ki te noho hei mana motuhake, hei mana whenua, ā, ko te whakapono hoki nā te iwi te mana whenua ehara nā te tangata kotahi.
reference: There are a lot of social and political effects such as the use of metric system, a shift from absolutism to republicanism, nationalism and the belief the country belongs to the people not to one sole ruler.

source: Me te mea hoki, whai muri i te Hurihanga i tuwhera ngā mahi ki ngā kaitono tāne katoa e āhei ai te hunga tīno māia, tīno kaha ki te toa.
reference: Also after the Revolution occupations were open to all male applicants allowing the most ambitious and successful to succeed.

source: Ka pērā hoki te ope tauā nā te mea ka whakaritea ngā akunga ope tauā i runga i te matatau, kaua ko te karangatanga.
reference: Same goes for the military because instead of army rankings being based on class they were now based on cailaber.

source: Nā te Pakanga a Wīwī i hihiri ai te hunga rawakore nō iwi kē ki te whawhai i ō rātou ake whahai.
reference: The French Revolution also inspired many other repressed working class people of other country's to began their own revolutions.

source: I tino ngākaunui a Muhammad ki ngā āhuatanga o te ao i tū atu i te ao kikokiko. I noho atu ia ki tētahi ana e karangatia nei ko "Hira," kei runga ake o te maunga o "Noor" (ko te whēriko), ki reira noho ai ki te āta whakaaro.
reference: Muhammad was deeply interested in matters beyond this mundane life. He used to frequent a cave that became known as “Hira‘” on the Mountain of “Noor” (light) for contemplation.

source: te ana ake, i mōrehurehu i aua wā, ka pūrangiaho te whakaahua o ngā aronga ā-wairua o Muhammad.
reference: he cave itself, which survived the times, gives a very vivid image of Muhammad’s spiritual inclinations.

source: E whakangā ana i te tihi o tētahi o ngā maunga kei te raki o Mecca, e tawhiti noa atu ana te ana mai i te toinga o te ao.
reference: Resting on the top of one of the mountains north of Mecca, the cave is completely isolated from the rest of the world.

source: Me te aha, ehara i te māmā te kite ahakoa i te mōhiotia kētia i reira tētahi. Ka tomo atu ana ki te ana, he mohoaotanga katoa.
reference: In fact, it is not easy to find at all even if one knew it existed. Once inside the cave, it is a total isolation.

source: Kāore he paku aha ka kitea tēnā i te rangi paki o runga me ngā tini pae maunga e karapoti nei. He iti noa o te ao nei ka kitea ka rangona rānei mai i roto i te ana.
reference: Nothing can be seen other than the clear, beautiful sky above and the many surrounding mountains. Very little of this world can be seen or heard from inside the cave.

source: Ko te Great Pyramid ki Giza te mōrehu kotahi anake e tū tonu ana i tēnei rā o ngā mea mīharo e whitu.
reference: The Great Pyramid at Giza is the only one of the seven wonders that is still standing today.

source: Nā te iwi o Īhipa i hanga i te rautau tuatoru BCE, ko te Kōeko Matua te kōeko i hangaia hei whakamaumaharatanga ki te Ihorei kua mate noa atu.
reference: Built by the Egyptians in the third century BCE, the Great Pyramid is one of many large pyramid structures built to honor dead Pharaoh.

source: Kei te mānia o Giza, "Giza Necropolis" rānei i te Valley of the Dead o Īhipa ētahi koeko (ko te koeke rahi te mea nui rawa), ētahi toma, ētahi temepara me te Sphinx hirahira.
reference: The Giza Plateau, or "Giza Necropolis" in the Egyptian Valley of the Dead contains several pyramids (of which the great pyramid is the largest), several small tombs, several temples, and the great Sphinx.

source: I hangaia te koeko mīharo hei whakanui i a Pharaoh Khufu, ā, he maha ngā koeko iti iho, ngā toma, ngā temepara anō hoki i hangaia hei whakanui i ā Khufu wāhine, whanaunga hoki.
reference: The great pyramid was created to honor the Pharaoh Khufu, and many of the smaller pyramids, tombs, and temples were built to honor Khufu's wives and family members.

source: Ko te āhua o te tohu "kōpere ki runga" ko tētahi V, ā, o te "tohu kōpere ki raro" ko tētahi tēpara, ko tētahi tapawhā ōrite me te taha whakararo e ngaro ana.
reference: The "up bow" mark looks like a V and the "down bow mark" like a staple or a square missing its bottom side.

source: Ko te tikanga o ki runga, me tīmata koe ki te hiku me te uaki i te kōpere, ā, ko te tikanga o ki raro, me tīmata koe ki te poraka (kei reira tō ringa e pupuri ana i te kōpere) me te tākiri i te kōpere.
reference: Up means you should start at the tip and push the bow, and down means you should start at the frog (which is where your hand is holding the bow) and pull the bow.

source: I te nuinga o te wā ka puta te oro māriri ake i tētahi kōpere-ki runga, kei kaha ake, rīrā ake ana te kōpere-ki raro.
reference: An up-bow usually generates a softer sound, while a down-bow is stronger and more assertive.

source: Tēnā kia mahea koe kia penerākaungia ō ake tohu, ēngari me maumahara ko ngā māka piko tā kei reira mō te take puoro, nōreira me kauanuanu ēnā.
reference: Feel free to pencil in your own marks, but remember the printed bowing marks are there for a musical reason, so they should usually be respected.

source: I te rā 6 o Ōketopa i te tau 1769 i panaia anō atu ki Parī i Versailles e tētahi māpu wāhine mākete te hunga tino mataku a King Louis XVI rātou ko Queen Marie Antoinette ko ā rāua tamariki nohinohi tokorua (a Marie Therese 11 tau te pakeke, rāua ko Louis-Charles e 4 tau te pakeke) ko te tuahine a te Kīngi a Madam Elizabeth.
reference: The terrified King Louis XVI, Queen Marie Antoinette their two young children (11 year old Marie Therese and four year old Louis-Charles) and the King's sister, Madam Elizabeth, on the 6th October 1789 were forced back to Paris from Versailles by a mob of market women.

source: Ki tētahi kāreti ka hoki atu rātou ki Parī e karapotia ana e te māpu tangata e tioro ana, e hāparangi ana i ngā whakatuma ki te Kīngi me te Kuini.
reference: In a carriage, they traveled back to Paris surrounded by a mob of people screaming and shouting threats against the King and Queen.

source: I tōpana te māpu tāngata nei i te Kīngi me te Kuīni kia tītoretore ngā matapihi o tō rāua kāreti.
reference: The mob of people forced the King And Queen to have their carriage windows wide open.

source: I tētahi wā i pōwhiriwhiritia e tētahi mema o te māpu i te upoko o tētahi tautiaki o te whare ariki i whakamatehia ki Versailles ki te aroaro o te Kuīni ihiihi.
reference: At one point a member of the mob waved the head of a royal guard killed at Versailles in front of the terrified Queen.

source: I utua ngā utu pakanga o te whakatuanui a te karauna o Ngā Whenua Tōpū o Amerika i te taeatanga o ngā Piripini e te hunga Piripini ake.
reference: The war expenditures of U.S. imperialism in the conquest of the Philippines were paid for by the Filipino people themselves.

source: I pēhia rātou kia utu tāke ki te whakahaere koroniara o Ngā Whenua Tōpū o Amerika hei tuku pūtea hei utu i tētahi wāhanga nui o ngā utu me te itareti o ngā hononga-moni kua whakarewaina ki te ingoa o te kāwanatanga Piripīni mā ngā whare pēke o Wall Street.
reference: They were compelled to pay taxes to the U.S. colonial regime to defray a major part of the expenditures and the interest on bonds floated in the name of the Philippine government through the Wall Street banking houses.

source: Āe rā, ko ngā whairawatanga e ahu mai ana i te aruarutanga o ngā iwi o Filipino e arotau noa ake ana i ngā āhuatanga whakatuatea ki Amerika.
reference: Of course, the superprofits derived from the protracted exploitation of the Filipino people would constitute the basic gains of U.S. imperialism.

source: Kia mārama ki ngā Templar me mārama te horopaki i tīmata i te hanganga o te rōpū.
reference: To understand the Templars one must understand the context that prompted the creation of the order.

source: Ko te wā i tū ai ngā mahi e kīia whānuitia ana ko ngā High Midle Ages arā te wā o te hītori Ūropi i ngā rautau 11, 12 me te 13 hoki (AD 1000-1300).
reference: The age where the events took place is commonly referred as the High Middle Ages the period of European history in the 11th, 12th, and 13th centuries (AD 1000–1300).

source: I mua i te High Middle Ages ko te Early Middle Ages, ā, ka whāia e te Late Middle Ages, e kīia ana i mutu tata ki te tau 1500.
reference: The High Middle Ages were preceded by the Early Middle Ages and followed by the Late Middle Ages, which by convention ends around 1500.

source: Ko te ākinga rangahau (technological determinism) tētahi rerenga e whakahāngai ana te whānuitanga o ngā ariā e whakamahia ana, mai i te pananga-hangarau, arā ko te pūtake hāngarau ko te huarahi a te tangata kei te kōkiritia e tētahi whakaaro whakakaupapa pūtake e hāngai ana ki ngā ture pūtaiao me ā rātou putanga ki te hangarau.
reference: Technological determinism is a term that encompasses a wide range of ideas in practice, from technology-push or the technological imperative to a strict sense that human destiny is driven by an underlying logic associated with scientific laws and their manifestation in technology.

source: E rua ngā whakaaro matua kei te nuinga o ngā whakamāramatanga o te mana motuhake hangarau: kei te whai te whanaketanga o te hangarau ake i tētahi huarahi i kō atu i te ahurea, i te whakaaweawe tōrangapū rānei, ā, he "pānga" ā-roto tonu tā te hangarau ki ngā pāori, tēnā i te heipūtanga o te tikanga ā-iwi.
reference: Most interpretations of technological determinism share two general ideas: that the development of technology itself follows a path largely beyond cultural or political influence, and that technology in turn has "effects" on societies that are inherent, rather than socially conditioned.

source: Hei tauira, ka kīia pea ka ara noa te motokā ki te whakawhanaketanga o ngā huarahi.
reference: For example, one might say that the motor car necessarily leads to the development of roads.

source: Heoi, ehara te kōtuitui huarahi ā-motu i te pai ā-ohaoha mō ētahi waka ruarua noa nei, nō reira ka whakawhanaketia ētahi rautaki hou mō te whakaputa hei whakaheke i te utu mō te hoko waka.
reference: However, a nationwide road network is not economically viable for just a handful of cars, so new methods of production are developed to reduce the cost of car ownership.

source: Mā te pupuri waka a te tokomaha e nui ake te tupono o ngā aituā ki ngā rori, mā korā te auahatanga o ngā tikanga hou ki te hauora mō te whakatika i ngā tinana kua whara.
reference: Mass car ownership also leads to a higher incidence of accidents on the roads, which leads to the invention of new techniques in healthcare for repairing damaged bodies.

source: I kaha te āhuatanga ahurea motuhaketanga i roto i te Romanticism, i māramatia mai i ngā kaituhi pēnei i a Goethe, i a Fichte, me Schlegel hoki.
reference: Romanticism had a large element of cultural determinism, drawn from writers such as Goethe, Fichte, and Schlegel.

source: Ki te horopaki o te Romanticism, nā te takotoranga papa te tangata i aweawe, ā, puta noa i te wā i hua mai ngā tikanga me te ahurea e hāngai ana ki tērā takotoranga papa, ā, e reretau ana ēnei ki te wāhi o te papori, nō reira he pai ake i ngā ture uruhi poka noa.
reference: In the context of Romanticism, the geography molded individuals, and over time customs and culture related to that geography arose, and these, being in harmony with the place of the society, were better than arbitrarily imposed laws.

source: Ka pērā i te āhua o te mōhiohio o Parī hei te tāone matua mō te kākahu rorotu o te ao o nāianei, i whai whakaarohia a Constantinople hei tāone matua mō te kākahu rorotu o Ūropi i neherā.
reference: In the manner that Paris is known as the fashion capital of the contemporary world, Constantinople was regarded as the fashion capital of feudal Europe.

source: E mōhio whānuitia ana hei pokapū o te hāneaneatanga, ā, i tīmata tana rongonui mō tērā i te takiwā o te A.D 400, ā, i haere tonu tae noa ki te A.D 1100.
reference: Its renown for being an epicenter of luxury began in about 400 A.D. and lasted up until about 1100 A.D.

source: Ka heke haere tōna tūnga i te wā o te rautau tua tekau mā rua i runga i te mōhio kua hoki mai ngā Crusaders whai koha pērā i ngā hiraka, i ngā rau kikini he nui ake te uara i tā ngā mākete Byzantine.
reference: Its status declined during the twelfth century mainly due to the fact that Crusaders had returned bearing gifts such as silks and spices that were valued more than what Byzantine markets offered.

source: I hua mai te taone matua whakamau pueru, mai i Constantinople ki Parī.
reference: It was at this time that the transfer of the title of Fashion Capital from Constantinople to Paris was made.

source: I rangatira rawa te kaupapa Gothic mai i te rautau 10 ki te rautau 11, me te rautau 14.
reference: Gothic style peaked in the period between the 10th - 11th centuries and the 14th century.

source: I te timatanga i whakaawengia te hanga kākahu e te ahurea Byzantine o te rāwhiti.
reference: At the beginning dress was heavily influenced by the Byzantine culture in the east.

source: Heoi, i i runga i ngā hononga whakawhiti kōrero pōturi, ka tōmuri mai pea ngā taera ki te uru mā te 25 ki te 30 tau.
reference: However, due to the slow communication channels, styles in the west could lag behind by 25 to 30 year.

source: tata ki te mutunga o ngā Middle Ages i tīmata a Ūropi ki te uru te whakawhanake i tā rātou ake huatau. ko tētahi o ngā whanaketanga nui rawa o te wā i hua mai i ngā ekenga ka tīmata ngā tāngata ki te whakamahi pātene hei whakamau kākahu.
reference: towards the end of the Middle Ages western Europe began to develop their own style. one of the biggest developments of the time as a result of the crusades people began to use buttons to fasten clothing.

source: Ko te ahuwhenua mō te hapori ko te ahuwhenua e whakahaerehia ana hei whakatipu i te rawaka o te kai hei tutuki anake i ngā hiahia o te kaiahuwhenua rātou ko tōna whānau.
reference: Subsistence agriculture is agriculture carried out for the production of enough food to meet just the needs of the agriculturalist and his/her family.

source: Ko te ahuwhenua-tiaki-ake he pūnaha māmā, i te nuinga o te wā he māori, e whakamahi ana i ngā kākano māori ki te wāhi pūnaha hauropi rā, e whakakotahitia ana ki te huringa hua, ki ētahi atu tikanga āhua māmā kia rahi ai ngā hua ka puta.
reference: Subsistence agriculture is a simple, often organic, system using saved seed native to the ecoregion combined with crop rotation or other relatively simple techniques to maximize yield.

source: I mua i te whai wāhi te nuinga o ngā kaipāmu ki te ahuwhenua whāngai i a rātou anō, ā, e pēnei tonu ana ki ngā tini whenua e whanake ana.
reference: Historically most farmers were engaged in subsistence agriculture and this is still the case in many developing nations.

source: Ka whakaemi ngā ahurea āpiti i ngā hunga e rite nei ngā whakaaro, te hunga kua peia atu e te pāpori, nā taea e rātou te whakawhanake i tō rātou ake āhua tuakiri.
reference: Subcultures bring together like-minded individuals who feel neglected by societal standards and allow them to develop a sense of identity.

source: Ka motuhake pea te ahurea-ā-hapū i te mea o te pakeketanga, o te iwi, o te akomanga, o te wāhi me te ira tangata o te mema hoki rānei.
reference: Subcultures can be distinctive because of the age, ethnicity, class, location, and/or gender of the members.

source: Ko ngā āhuatanga ka whakatau he rerekē te ahurea ā-hapū ko te reo, ko te āhuatanga, te hāhi, te tōrangapū, te aitanga, te takoto whenua, te kōnanunanutanga rānei o ētahi mea.
reference: The qualities that determine a subculture as distinct may be linguistic, aesthetic, religious, political, sexual, geographical, or a combination of factors.

source: He auau te tohu a ngā mema o tētahi ahurea āpiti i tā rātou whai wāhitanga mā tētahi whakamahinga āhua he rerekē, he tohu, kei roto ko ngā āhua, ngā tū, ko ngā huinga kupu.
reference: Members of a subculture often signal their membership through a distinctive and symbolic use of style, which includes fashions, mannerisms, and argot.

source: Ko tētahi o ngā tīno rautaki e whakamahia ai hei whakatauira i te hirahiratanga o te whakahoahoa he kōrero mō ngā āhuatanga pōuri o ngā tamariki i waiho kaungia, i harangia, i patungia rānei, i kore rānei i tīakina e ngā mātua i tō rātou ohinga ake.
reference: One of the most common methods used to illustrate the importance of socialization is to draw upon the few unfortunate cases of children who were, through neglect, misfortune, or wilful abuse, not socialized by adults while they were growing up.

source: Kua kīia ngā tamariki pēnei he "kūao" he mohoao rānei. Ko ētahi tamariki kūao kua herea e te tangata (i te nuinga o te wā ko ō rātou ake mātua); i ētahi wā i ahu mai tēnei whakarerenga tamaiti i te pana a ngā mātua i te tino hauātanga ā-hinengaro, ā-tinana rānei a te tamaiti.
reference: Such children are called "feral" or wild. Some feral children have been confined by people (usually their own parents); in some cases this child abandonment was due to the parents' rejection of a child's severe intellectual or physical impairment.

source: Akuanei pea i pāngia ngā tamariki mohowao e te whakamanioro, e te kino i mua i te waiho kau, i te omanga atu rānei.
reference: Feral children may have experienced severe child abuse or trauma before being abandoned or running away.

source: Kua whakapaetia ētahi atu kua whakatipuria e te kararehe; e kīia ana kua noho mohoao ētahi ki tōna kotahi.
reference: Others are alleged to have been brought up by animals; some are said to have lived in the wild on their own.

source: Ina whakatipuria katoatia e te kararehe ehara i te tangata, ka whakaatu te tamaiti mohoao i ngā whanonga (i roto tonu i ngā here ā-kiko) kei te pērā katoa i ērā o te kararehe-tiaki ake, pērā i tana mataku, tana kore aro rānei ki te tangata.
reference: When completely brought up by non-human animals, the feral child exhibits behaviors (within physical limits) almost entirely like those of the particular care-animal, such as its fear of or indifference to humans.

source: Ahakoa me māmā ake, me manawa reka ake hoki te ako mā te ako pūtake kaupapa, ka neke atu te whakatīrewa i tērā.
reference: While project based learning should make learning easier and more interesting, scaffolding goes a step beyond.

source: Ehara te tīrewa i tētahi tikanga hou o te ako, engari kē ia he mea tautoko e tuku ana i te tautoko ki ngā takitahi e whai ana i tētahi wheako ako hou, pērā i te whakamahi i tētahi pūmanawa rorohiko hou, te tīmata rānei i tētahi kaupapa hou.
reference: Scaffolding is not a method of learning but rather an aid that provides support to individuals whom are undergoing a new learning experience such as using a new computer program or beginning a new project.

source: Ka taea ngā tīrewa ā-mariko, ā-kiko hoki, arā, he momo tīrewa te kaiako engari ka pērā hoki te tangata pine pepa paku ki Microsoft Office.
reference: Scaffolds can be both virtual and real, in other words, a teacher is a form of scaffold but so is the little paperclip man in Microsoft Office.

source: Kei te hangaia ki roto i te pūmanawa ngā Tīrewa Mariko (Virtual Scaffolds), ā, ko tā rātou mahi he ui, he whāngai kupu, he whakamārama hoki i ngā hātepe kua uaua rawa mā te ākonga te whakahaere i tōna kotahi.
reference: Virtual Scaffolds are internalized in the software and are meant to question, prompt, and explain procedures that may have been to challenging for the student to handle alone.

source: He nui ngā āhuatanga e whāngaihia ai te tamaiti, mai i te korenga o ōna mātua e manaaki i a ia ki ngā mahi tūkino tamariki, tae atu ki ngā mahi nanakia hoki.
reference: Children are placed in Foster Care for a wide variety of reasons that range from neglect, to abuse, and even to extortion.

source: Me kaua rawa tētahi tamaiti e tipu ake ki te taiao kāore i te noho taurima, āroharoha, whakaako hoki, engari anō ka pērā.
reference: No child should ever have to grow up in an environment that is not nurturing, caring, and educational, but they do.

source: E whakaaro ana mātou he wāhi haumaru te Foster Care System mā ēnei tamariki.
reference: We perceive the Foster Care System to be a safety zone for these children.

source: Ko te tikanga he kāinga haumaru ngā kainga tiaki whāngai, he mātua āroha hoki, he whai mātauranga pai, he pai hoki te taha hauora.
reference: Our foster care system is supposed to provide safe homes, loving caregivers, stable education, and reliable health care.

source: Ko te tikanga , ka pai ake te noho a te whāngai ki ēnei kāinga i ngā kainga i noho ai rātou.
reference: Foster care is supposed to provide all the necessities that were lacking in the home they were previously taken from.

source: Kei te whakakotahi te Ipurangi i ngā pūmotu o te papatipu me te whakawhitinga whaiaro anō hoki.
reference: The Internet combines elements of both mass and interpersonal communication.

source: Ko ngā āhuahanga rerekē o te Ipurangi ka ar atu ki ngā āhuatanga tāpiri e pā an ki te aronga whakamahi me te ngata.
reference: The distinct characteristics of the Internet lead to additional dimensions in terms of the uses and gratifications approach.

source: Hei tauira, e marohitia ana te "ako" me te "pāhekoheko" hei whakahihikotanga hira mō te whakamahinga Ipurangi (James et al., 1995).
reference: For example, “learning” and “socialization” are suggested as important motivations for Internet use (James et al., 1995).

source: Ko te "whakauru takitahi" me te "tōai whanaungatanga" i tautohua hei mea whakaaweawe hou mai i a Eighmey me McCord (1998) i te wā rātou i whakawā i ngā putanga o ngā minenga i te ipurangi.
reference: “Personal involvement” and “continuing relationships” were also identified as new motivation aspects by Eighmey and McCord (1998) when they investigated audience reactions to websites.

source: Kua ahu mai i te whakamahinga o te mau ataata ki ngā kitenga whakahirahira ki te whakamāramatanga o ngā ihiihi-paku, arā ko ngā nekehanga kanohi he mirihēkona ruarua noa te roa.
reference: The use of video recording has led to important discoveries in the interpretation of micro-expressions, facial movements which last a few milliseconds.

source: Inarā, e kīia ana ka taea te kite mēnā kei te rūkahu tētahi tangata mā te whakamārama tika i ngā ihiihi paku rawa.
reference: In particular, it is claimed that one can detect whether a person is lying by interpreting micro-expressions correctly.

source: I roto i tā Oliver Sacks pepa The President's Speech, e kōrero ana mō te nui o ngā tāngata kāore e mārama ki te kōrero ko te mate roro te take ka taea tonu e rātou te mārama tika ki ngā kare-ā-roto.
reference: Oliver Sacks, in his paper The President's Speech, indicated how people who are unable to understand speech because of brain damage are nevertheless able to assess sincerity accurately.

source: Kei te marohi tonu ia ko ngā āheinga pērā mō te whakamārama i ngā whanonga ā-tāngata ka noho tahi hoki ki ngā kararehe pērā i ngā kurī whakarata.
reference: He even suggests that such abilities in interpreting human behavior may be shared by animals such as domestic dogs.

source: Kua whakaaturia e te rangahau o te rautau rua tekau e rua ngā rōpū o te rerenga ira tangata: ko te mea huna, ko te mea puaki.
reference: Twentieth century research has shown that there are two pools of genetic variation: hidden and expressed.

source: Mā te irakē e tāpiri te rerenga ira hou, ā, mā te kōwhiringa e wewete mai i te puna o te rerenga e putaputa ana.
reference: Mutation adds new genetic variation, and selection removes it from the pool of expressed variation.

source: Ko te whakatāuki me te whakakotahi anō ka tuku atu, ka tuku mai i te rerenga i waenganui i ngā rōpū e rua ki ia reanga.
reference: Segregation and recombination shuffle variation back and forth between the two pools with each generation.

source: I roto i te Savanna, he uaua mō te whāngote waewae-rua he pūnaha nakunaku kai tōnā pēnei i te tangata kia pai ai tana kapo i ngā waikawa amino mai i ngā hua o te tipu.
reference: Out on the savanna, it is hard for a primate with a digestive system like that of humans to satisfy its amino-acid requirements from available plant resources.

source: Waihoki, ki te kore pērā ka taumaha ngā hua: ka pēhia te tipunga, ka ngoikore te taikaha, ā, nā wai rā ka hemo.
reference: Moreover, failure to do so has serious consequences: growth depression, malnutrition, and ultimately death.

source: Ko te hua o te rākau e tino wātea ana ko ngā pūmua kei roto i ngā rau me ngā rākau pī, heoi he uaua mō ngā whāngote waewae-rua pēnei i a tātou te nakunaku i ēnei kai ki te kore e tunua.
reference: The most readily accessible plant resources would have been the proteins accessible in leaves and legumes, but these are hard for primates like us to digest unless they are cooked.

source: Hei whakatairitenga, ehara i te mea he māmā anake ki te nakunaku i te kai kararehe (pōpokorua, ngārara, hēkī) engari he nui hoki te pūmua o roto me ngā waikawa amino waiwai katoa.
reference: In contrast, animal foods (ants, termites, eggs) not only are easily digestible, but they provide high-quantity proteins that contain all the essential amino acids.

source: I te mutunga iho, me kaua tātou e ohorere mēnā i whakaea ō tātou ake tūpuna i tā rātou "raru pūmua" āhua pērā i tā ngā makimaki mōhio ki te whenua hawana (savanna) i tēnei wā.
reference: All things considered, we should not be surprised if our own ancestors solved their "protein problem" in somewhat the same way that chimps on the savanna do today.

source: Ko te kokoti moe ko te hātepe o te āta whakaoho i te wā noa o tō moe, me te hoki ki te moe tētahi wā poto whai muri ake (10-60 ngā meneti).
reference: Sleep interruption is the process of purposefully awakening during your normal sleep period and falling asleep a short time later (10–60 minutes).

source: Ka māmā te mahi pēnei mā te whakamahi i tētahi matawā whakaara āhua ngū kia tō mai koe ki te ao mārama karekau te oho katoa.
reference: This can be easily done by using a relatively quiet alarm clock to bring you to consciousness without fully waking you.

source: Ki te kite koe i tō whakarite anō i te karaka i te wā e moe ana koe, ka taea te whakatakoto ki tērā atu taha o te rūma, hei reira koe me peke atu i te moenga hei weto.
reference: If you find yourself resetting the clock in your sleep, it can be placed on the other side of the room, forcing you to get out of bed to turn it off.

source: Ētahi kē atu kōwhiringa o te mahi i runga i te rere o te rā ko te inu i te nui o ngā wē (inarā te wai, te tī rānei, pai mō te whakaputa mimi) i mua i te moe, nō reira me maranga te tangata ki te mimi.
reference: Other biorhythm-based options involve drinking lots of fluid (particularly water or tea, a known diuretic) prior to sleep, forcing one to get up to urinate.

source: E hāngai tauaro ana te rahi o te rongo ā-roto o te tangata ki te rahi o te tanuku ki te tinana, te manawa pā ki te wairua hoki.
reference: The amount of inner peace a person possesses correlates oppositely to the amount of tension in one’s body and spirit.

source: Nā te iti ake o te mauri ka nui ake ai te mauri pai o koneki. Kei ia tangata te pito mata kia rapu i te rangimarie me te māuruuru roa.
reference: The lower the tension, the more positive the life force present. Every person has the potential to find absolute peace and contentment.

source: Ka taea e te katoa te tae atu ki te māramatanga. Ko te ārai anake e tū ana ki te ara o tēnei whāinga ko tō tātou ake manawa pā, whakakāhoretanga hoki.
reference: Everyone can achieve enlightenment. The only thing standing in the way of this goal is our own tension and negativity.

source: E hāngai ana te Tibetan Buddhism ki ā Buddha whakaakoranga, engari i whakawhānuingia e te ara aroha o mahayana me ngā tini āhua ā-mahi o te ioka nō Īnia.
reference: The Tibetan Buddhism is based on the teachings of Buddha, but were extended by the mahayana path of love and by a lot of techniques from Indian Yoga.

source: E mārama ana te Tibetan Buddhism. Ko te Kundalini Yoga, ko te whakamanatu me te huarahi mātua ki te aroha mutunga-kore.
reference: In principle the Tibetan Buddhism is very simple. It consists of Kundalini Yoga, meditation and the path of all-embracing love.

source: Ki te Kundalini Yoga ka whakaohotia te ngao Kundalini (te ngao māramatanga) mā ngā tūnga ioka, ngā mahi hā, ngā kīanga whakatau, me te aronga ki ngā wawata.
reference: With Kundalini Yoga the Kundalini energy (enlightenment energy) is awakened through yoga postures, breathing exercises, mantras and visualizations.

source: Ko te pokapū o te whakatau whakaaro Tipete ko te Deity Yoga. Mā te whakaaro ā-ataata ki ngā tini atua ka whakawāteatia ngā ia ngoi, ka kāngia ngā hakara (chakra), ā, ka hangaia te māramatanga.
reference: The center of Tibetan meditation is the Deity Yoga. Through the visualization of various deities the energy channels are cleaned, the chakras are activated and the enlightenment consciousness is created.

source: He hoariri whānui a Tiamani i te Pakanga Tuarua o te Ao, i runga i tērā ka mahi tahi a USSR me Ngā Whenua Tōpū o Amerika. I te mutunga o te pakanga ka pukuriri ēnei whenua ki a rāua anō i te mea o te tūtukitanga o te pūnaha, o te hātepe me te ahurea i pēnei ai.
reference: Germany was a common enemy in World War 2, leading to cooperation between the USSR and USA. With the end of the war the clashes of system, process and culture led to the countries falling out.

source: I te rua tau o te mutunga o te pakanga, he hoariri ngā hoa o mua ināianei, ā, i tīmata te Cold War.
reference: With two years of the end of the war, the former allies were now enemies and the Cold War began.

source: Ka tū mō te 40 tau i te whai ake nei, ā, ka tūturu te whawhai, e ngā ope tauā whakakapi, ki ngā papariri atu i Āwherika ki Āhia, ki Afghanistan, ki Kīupa, ki ngā tini whenua anō.
reference: It was to last for the next 40 years and would be fought for real, by proxy armies, on battlefields from Africa to Asia, in Afghanistan, Cuba and many other places.

source: Ka tae ki te 17 o Hepetema, ka pakaru kē atu te pae ārai Polish, ko te tūmanako nui kia hoki anō, ka whakatika ake anō ki ngā whakawhitinga o Rōmania.
reference: By September 17, 1939, the Polish defense was already broken, and the only hope was to retreat and reorganise along the Romanian bridgehead.

source: Heoi anō, i noho korehāhā ēnei mahere i te tata ki te kotahi pō, i te kuhu atu me te hanga a te neke atu i te 800,000 hoia i te Red Army a te Soviet Union i ngā pae Belarussian me te Ukranian i muri i te eke ki ngā rohe rāwhiti o Pōrana me te takahi i te Riga Peace Treaty, te Soviet-Polish Non-Aggression Pact, me ētahi atu tiriti ā-ao, i waenganui i ngā whenua e rua, whenua takitini rānei.
reference: However, these plans were rendered obsolete nearly overnight, when over 800,000 soldiers from the Soviet's Union Red Army entered and created the Belarussian and Ukrainian fronts after invading the eastern regions of Poland in violation of the Riga Peace Treaty, the Soviet-Polish Non-Aggression Pact, and other international treaties, both bilateral and multilateral.

source: Ko te whakamahinga o te kaipuke te rautaki pai rawa kia kawe i ngā tini tāngata me ngā rawa i te moana.
reference: Using ships to transport goods is by far the most efficient way to move large amounts of people and goods across oceans.

source: Ko te mahi a te ope tauā moana he whakarite kia āhei ai tō whenua te āheinga kia neke i ō tāngata me ngā rawa, ā, i te wā orite e whakararu ana i ā te hoariri āheinga ki te neke i āna tāngata me āna rawa.
reference: The job of navies has traditionally been to ensure that your country maintains the ability to move your people and goods, while at the same time, interfering with your enemy's ability to move his people and goods.

source: Ko tētahi o ngā tauira whakamiramira nō nākuanei o tēnei ko te kauhanga riri North Atlantic o Te Pakanga Tuarua o te Ao. I ngana ngā Amerikana ki te whakaneke i ngā tāne, i ngā rawa whakawhiti atu i te Atlantic Ocean hei āwhina i a Peretānia.
reference: One of the most noteworthy recent examples of this was the North Atlantic campaign of WWII. The Americans were trying to move men and materials across the Atlantic Ocean to help Britain.

source: I taua wā tonu, i te ngana te tauā-moana o Tiamani te whakatū i te hohoko mā te whakamahi i ngā poti-U.
reference: At the same time, the German navy, using mainly U-boats, was trying to stop this traffic.

source: Mena kua i hinga ngā hoawhawhai, Kua kore pea e riro i a Tiamana a Piritania pēnei i te rironga o te toinga o Europi.
reference: Had the Allies failed, Germany probably would have been able to conquer Britain as it had the rest of Europe.

source: Ko te āhua nei i whakararatatia ngā nanekoti tata ki te 10,000 tau ki muri ki ngā Zagros Mountains ki Irāna.
reference: Goats seem to have been first domesticated roughly 10,000 years ago in the Zagros Mountains of Iran.

source: Ka tīmata ngā ahurea me ngā iwi tawhito te pupuri i ērā kia māmā ai te āheinga atu ki te miraka, te huruhuru, te kiko, me ngā kirimoko.
reference: Ancient cultures and tribes began to keep them for easy access to milk, hair, meat, and skins.

source: I rāhuitia ngā koti ki tētahi kāhui karore haere i ngā maunga me ngā pātiki, i manaakitia ai e te hunga tamariki, tōna rite ki te hēpara. E whakamahia tonutia ana ēnei tikanga ārahi kararehe i ēnei rā.
reference: Domestic goats were generally kept in herds that wandered on hills or other grazing areas, often tended by goatherds who were frequently children or adolescents, similar to the more widely known shepherd. These methods of herding are still used today.

source: I hangaia ngā kāta ki Ingaringi i te tīmatanga o te rautau 16.
reference: Wagonways were built in England as early as the 16th Century.

source: Akakoa ko ngā ara wākena ko ngā papa rākau whakarara noa iho, i whakahei rātou i ngā hoiho e tō ana i a rātou te eke ki ngā tere nui ake me te tō i ngā wahanga nui ake i tērā ki ngā huarahi tuaranga o te wā.
reference: Although wagonways merely consisted of parallel planks of wood, they allowed horses pulling them to achieve greater speeds and pull larger loads than on the slightly more rough roads of the day.

source: I whakatō āhua moata ngā Crossties kia mau pū i ngā ara ki tana wāhi. Nāwai rā, heoi anō, i āta kitea i te painga kē atu o ngā ara mēnā i he wāhanga mētara iti ki runga.
reference: Crossties were introduced fairly early to hold the tracks in place. Gradually, however, it was realised that tracks would be more efficient if they had a stip of iron on the top.

source: I tū tēnei hei tikanga noa, engari nā te rino i kaha ake te taiākotikoti ki ngā wīra wāpu o ngā wēkana.
reference: This became common practice, but the iron caused more wear on the wooden wheels of the wagons.

source: Tāria te wā, i whakakapia ngā wīra rākau ki ngā wīra rino. I te tau 1767, i kitea tuatahitia ngā ara tereina tuatahi he rino-katoa.
reference: Eventually, wooden wheels were replaced by iron wheels. In 1767, the first full-iron rails were introduced.

source: Ko te hīkoi te ikiiki tuatahi i mōhiotia, i tīmata tā te tangata hīkoi matika e rua miriona tau ki muri, i te aranga mai o te Homo Erectus (e kī ana te tangata matika).
reference: The first known transportation was walking, humans began walking upright two million years ago with the emergence of Homo Erectus (meaning upright man).

source: Ō rātou whakamuanga, ngā Australopithecus, kāore i pēnā rawa te hīkoi whakarunga i ngā wā katoa.
reference: Their predecessors, the Australopithecus did not walk upright as habitually.

source: E kitea ana ngā āhuatanga rua-waewae i roto i ngā mātātoka Australopithecus mai i ngā tau 4.2-3.9 miriona ki muri, he ahakoa i hīkoi pea te Sahelanthropus ki ngā waewae e rua e whitu miriona tau ki muri.
reference: Bipedal specializations are found in Australopithecus fossils from 4.2-3.9 million years ago, although Sahelanthropus may have walked on two legs as early as seven million years ago.

source: Ka taea e tātou te tīmata ki te noho pai ake me te taiao, ka taea e tātou te tūhono ki te kotahitanga mō te taiao, ka taea hoki e tātou te tū hei kaiwhakatūtū kia āhua whakahiki i te pākatokato hei te wā e heke mai ana.
reference: We can start living more friendly to the environment, we can join to the environmental movement, and we can even be activists in order to reduce the future suffering in some degree.

source: E ōrite ana tēnei ki te whakamaimoatanga o ngā tohumate i ētahi wā. Heoi anō, ki te kore tātou e hiahia ki te rongoā taupua noa iho, me rapu tātou i te aka o ngā raru, ā, me whakakore e tātou.
reference: This is just like symptomatic treatment in many cases. However, if we do not only want a temporary solution, then we should find the root of the problems, and we should deactivate them.

source: E mārama ana kua huri nei ao i ngā mahi a te ira tangata ā-pūtaiao nei, ā-hangarau nei hoki, ā, kua nui kē atu ngā raruraru kua pā mai i te kōpā rawa o te noho a ngā iwi i te ātaahuatanga a te noho a tatou te tangata.
reference: It is obvious enough that the world has changed much because of humankind's scientific and technological advancements, and problems have become greater because of overpopulation and mankind's extravagant lifestyle.

source: Whai muri i tana whakaaetanga e te Congress i te rā 4 o Hūrae, ka tukuna tētahi tuhinga hukihuki kua hainatia e te President of Congress a John Hancock rāua ko Secretary Charles Thompson ētahi poraka atu ki te toa tā a John Dunlap.
reference: After its adoption by Congress on July 4, a handwritten draft signed by the President of Congress John Hancock and the Secretary Charles Thomson was then sent a few blocks away to the printing shop of John Dunlap.

source: I roto i te pō i waenganui i te 150 me te 200 ngā kape i hangaia, e mōhiotia ana ināianei ko ngā "Dunlap broadsides".
reference: Through the night between 150 and 200 copies were made, now known as "Dunlap broadsides".

source: Nā John Nixon te pānuitanga tūmatanui tuatahi o te puka ki te iari o te Independence Hall i te 8 o Hūrae.
reference: The first public reading of the document was by John Nixon in the yard of Independence Hall on July 8.

source: I tukuna tētahi ki a George Washington i te 6 o Hūrae, ka pānuitia ki ōna hōia i New york i te 9 o Hūrae. I tae he kape ki Rānana i te 10 o Akuhata.
reference: One was sent to George Washington on July 6, who had it read to his troops in New York on July 9. A copy reached London on August 10.

source: Ko ngā Dunlap taha-whānui (Broadsides) e 25 e mōhiotia tonutia ana ko ngā tāruatanga tawhiti rawa e toe ana o te puka. Kāore te tuhinga tūturu ā-ringa i te toe tonu.
reference: The 25 Dunlap broadsides still known to exist are the oldest surviving copies of the document. The original handwritten copy has not survived.

source: Tokomaha ngā tohunga mātātoka i tēnei wā e whakapono ana ko tētahi rōpū mokonui i mōrehurehu, ā, kei te ora tonu i tēnei wā. E kīia ana e tātou he manu.
reference: Many paleontologists today believe that one group of dinosaurs survived and is alive today. We call them birds.

source: He tini ngā tāngata kāore e whakapop ana he tainahoro ērā rā i te mea he huruhuru ōna, ā, e taea te rere.
reference: Many people don't think about them as dinosaurs because they have feathers and can fly.

source: He tini ngā mea o te manu e rite tonu nei ki te tainahora.
reference: But there are a lot of things about birds that still look like a dinosaur.

source: He waewae unaunahi me te matikuku, ka whakawhānau hēki, ā, ka hīkoi i runga i ōna waewae ō muri pēnei i te T-Rex.
reference: They have feet with scales and claws, they lay eggs, and they walk on their two back legs like a T-Rex.

source: I te nuinga o te wā ko ngā rorohiko e whakamahia nei i ēnei rā nei kua hanga anō he rāwekeweke i ngā pitopito kōrero kua hangaia i roto i ngā āhuatanga o ngā nama tāhūrua.
reference: Virtually all computers in use today are based on the manipulation of information which is coded in the form of binary numbers.

source: Ka taea anake e tētahi tau tāhūrua te whai i tētahi o ngā uara e rua, arā te 0, te 1 rānei, ā, e kīia ana ēnei tau hei mati tāhūrua - hei moka ranei, kia whakamahia te kupu rorohiko.
reference: A binary number can have only one of two values, i.e. 0 or 1, and these numbers are referred to as binary digits - or bits, to use computer jargon.

source: Kāore pea te paihana ō roto e kitea inamatangia. Ko ngā tohu mate, pēnei i te ruaki he noa te āhua kia kore e taea te whakatau māuiui i taua wā tonu.
reference: Internal poisoning may not be immediately apparent. Symptoms, such as vomiting are sufficiently general that an immediate diagnosis cannot be made.

source: Ko te tohu pai rawa o te paihana ā-roto ko te noho ki reira a tētahi ipu tuwhera o te rongoā, o ngā matū ā-whare tāoke rānei.
reference: The best indication of internal poisoning may be the presence of an open container of medication or toxic household chemicals.

source: Tirohia te tapanga mō ngā tohutohu āwhina tuatahi mō taua paihana motuhake.
reference: Check the label for specific first aid instructions for that specific poison.

source: Ko te kupu ngārara, e ai ki ngā tohunga ngārara mō tēnei momo horopaki, he pēpeke.
reference: The term bug is used by entomologists in a formal sense for this group of insects.

source: Kei te ahu mai tēnei kupu i te waiatanga tahito ki ngā Bed-bugs, arā, he pepeke kua tino urutau ki te noho pirinoa ki te tangata.
reference: This term derives from ancient familiarity with Bed-bugs, which are insects highly adapted to parasitize humans.

source: Ka whānau pangore mai ngā Assassin-bugs me ngā Bed-bugs, kua whanaketia kia noho ki te kōhanga, ki te kāinga rānei o tō rātou rauropi papa.
reference: Both Assassin-bugs and Bed-bugs are nidicolous, adapted to living in nest or housing of their host.

source: Puta noa i Ngā Whenua Tōpū o Amerika he tata ki te 400,000 kēhi e mōhiotia ana o te Multiple Sclerosis (MS), nō reira ko ia te tahumaero ā-roro matua ki ngā pakeke he rangatahi, he waenga rānei te pakeke.
reference: Across the United States of America, there are approximately 400,000 known cases of Multiple Sclerosis (MS), leaving it as the leading neurological disease in younger and middle aged adults.

source: Ko MS tētahi tahumaero e pā ana ki te pūnaha ioio matua, e hangaia ana ki te roro, te aho tuaiwi me te ioio mai i te roro ki te karu.
reference: MS is a disease that affects the central nervous system, which is made up of the brain, the spinal cord and the optic nerve.

source: Kua kitea e te rangahau ko te tupono kia pāngia te wahine e te MS e rua whakareatanga ki tā te tāne.
reference: Research has found that females are two times more likely to have MS then males.

source: Ka whakatau pea tētahi tokorua kāore i te whai tikanga pai rawa mō rāua, mō tā rāua tamaiti rānei, te whakatipu pēpi.
reference: A couple may decide it is not in their best interest, or in the interest of their child, to raise a baby.

source: Ka kōwhiri pea ēnei tokorua te hanga i tētahi mahere whāngai mō tā rāua pēpi.
reference: These couples may choose to make an adoption plan for their baby.

source: Ki te whāngai, ka motu ngā mātua whakawhānau i ō rātou motika ā-mātua kia whakatipuria te tamaiti e tētahi atu tokorua.
reference: In an adoption, the birth parents terminate their parental rights so that another couple may parent the child.

source: Ko te whāinga matua a te pūtaiao he mōhio ki te mahinga o te ao mā te tikanga pūtaiao. Ko tēnei tikanga nei hei arataki i te nuinga o ngā rangahau pūtaiao.
reference: Science’s main goal is to figure out the way the world works through the scientific method. This method in fact guides most scientific research.

source: Ehara i te mea ko ia ki tōna kotahi, te mātaitanga, ā, ko te mātaitanga ko tētahi whakamātautau e whakamahia ana hei whakakore i te whakapae kotahi, neke atu rānei ka taea, te ui i ngā pātai, me te whakatātare hei ārahi hoki i ngā rangahau pūtaiao.
reference: It isn’t alone though, experimentation, and an experiment is a test that is used to eliminate one or more of the possible hypotheses, asking questions, and making observations also guide scientific research.
