source: I aro te hunga aro taiao me ngā wānanga ki ngā pukapuka o nehe, inarā, ki te paipera ki te reo Rātini.
reference: Naturalists and philosophers focused on classical texts and, in particular, on the Bible in Latin.

source: I whakaaetia ngā tirohanga o Aristotle ki ngā kaupapa katoa o te pūtaiao, tae atu ki te mātai hinengaro.
reference: Accepted were Aristotle's views on all matters of science, including psychology.

source: I te heke haere o te reo Kariki, ka haukotia te Uru i ōna pakiaka wānanga, pūtaiao Kariki hoki.
reference: As knowledge of Greek declined, the West found itself cut off from its Greek philosophical and scientific roots.

source: He rahi tonu ngā tāngata i mātai i te manawataki o te koiora, e mārama ai ki ngā whanonga me tirohia ngā huringa o te tinana me ngā hua ka puta i ngā mahi a te tinana.
reference: Many observed rhythms in physiology and behavior often crucially depend on the presence of endogenous cycles and their production through biological clocks.

source: Ko ngā ia ā-wā, arā, ehara ēnei i ngā urupare noa iho ki ngā tohu ā-wā ki waho, kua kitea mō te tata ki te katoa o ngā koiora, tae atu ki ngā huakita, ngā hekaheka, ngā tipu, ngā kararehe anō hoki.
reference: Periodic rhythms, which are not simply responses to external periodic cues, have been documented for most living beings, including bacteria, fungi, plants, and animals.

source: Ko ngā karaka koiora he mea ngapu kei te tautīnei i a rātou anō ka haere tonu te wā o te huringa rere noa ahakoa kei te ngaro ngā tohu nō waho.
reference: Biological clocks are self sustaining oscillators which will continue a period of free-running cycling even in the absence of external cues.

source: Ko te whakamātautau Hershey me Chase tētahi o ngā whakaaro i whakatau ai he mea ira te pītau ira.
reference: The Hershey and Chase experiment was one of the leading suggestions that DNA was a genetic material.

source: I whakamahingia e Hershey me Chase ngā wāhanga, ngā huaketo rānei hei whakauru i ō rātou ake pītau ira ki roto ki tētahi kitakita.
reference: Hershey and Chase used phages, or viruses, to implant their own DNA into a bacterium.

source: I whakamahia e rātau ētahi whakamātautau e rua e tohu ana i te pītau ira ki te huaketo he phage te momo me tētahi hīnātore iraruke, ki te pūmua o te huaketo phage me te pūngāwhā iraruke rānei.
reference: They did two experiments marking either the DNA in the phage with a radioactive phosphorus or the protein of the phage with radioactive sulfur.

source: He maha ngā momo iranoi, kei runga i tōna momo e kitea ai ka pēhea e raru ai, ko te hihiri o te momo ira kua pāngia, ā, mēnā rānei he momo meroiti ngā ira e pāngia ana.
reference: Mutations can have a variety of different effects depending on the type of mutation, the significance of the piece of genetic material affected and whether the cells affected are germ-line cells.

source: Ko ngā rerekētanga anake ki ngā raina-ira ka tukuna ki te hunga tamariki, ko ētahi atu rā ka hua mai te mate-ira, te mate pukupuku rānei.
reference: Only mutations in germ-line cells can be passed on to children, while mutations elsewhere can cause cell-death or cancer.

source: Kei te whakamanea te tāpoitanga ki te taiao i ngā tāngata e manawa reka ana te toro atu ki ngā wāhi māori mō te ngahau i te tirohanga te take, tae atu ki ngā tipu, ki ngā kararehe.
reference: Nature-based tourism attracts people interested in visiting natural areas for the purpose of enjoying the scenery, including plant and animal wildlife.

source: Hei whakatauira ake i ngā mahi ki konei, ko te aruaru, ko te hī ika, ko ngā mahi tango whakaahua, ko te mātai manu, ko te tirotiro whenua me te ako i ngā mōhiotanga e pā ana ki te taiao.
reference: Examples of on-site activities include hunting, fishing, photography, bird watching, and visiting parks and studying information about the ecosystem.

source: Ko tētahi tauira ko te toro, te mau whakaahua me te ako e pā ana ki ngā orangitangi ki Borneo.
reference: An example is visiting, photographing, and learning about organgatuangs in Borneo.

source: Ia ata, ka wehewehe ngā tāngata i ngā tāone iti ki taiwhenua ki ngā waka ki te haere ki tō rātou wāhi mahi, ā, ka whakawhititia e ētahi atu ko tō rātou wāhi mahi ko te wāhi kātahi anō rātou ka wehe.
reference: Every morning, people leave small country towns in cars to go their workplace and are passed by others whose work destination is the place they have just left.

source: I roto i tēnei pahi kawekawe uekaha nei kua honotahi, kua tautoko hoki te katoa i tētahi pūnaha kawenga e hāngai ana ki ngā waka tūmatawhāiti.
reference: In this dynamic transport shuttle everyone is somehow connected with, and supporting, a transport system based on private cars.

source: Ināianei, e tohu ana te pūtaiao ko te ohanga nui mō te waro kei te tūraki i te rangipūātea mai i tōna mauri tau, i manaakitia ai te ira tangata mō te 2 miriona tau ki muri.
reference: Science now indicates that this massive carbon economy has dislodged the biosphere from one of its stable states that has supported human evolution for the past two million years.

source: Ka whai wāhi atu te kato ki ngā pūnaha mauranga. Ko te nuiga ka amuamu mō ngā pūnaha mauranga.
reference: Everyone participates in society and uses transportation systems. Almost everyone complains about transportation systems.

source: I ngā whenua kua whanake, me uaua ka rongo koe i ngā momo amuamu mō te pai o te wai, mō te turakitanga rānei o ngā piriti.
reference: In developed countries you seldom hear similar levels of complaints about water quality or bridges falling down.

source: He aha ka puta mai ngā amuamu pērā i ngā pūnaha ikiiki, ā, he aha ka hinga i ia rā? He koretake noa ngā kaipūkaha ikiiki? He take pūtake ake kei te haere rānei?
reference: Why do transportation systems engender such complaints, why do they fail on a daily basis? Are transportation engineers just incompetent? Or is something more fundamental going on?

source: Ko Traffic Flow te mātai i te rerenga o ngā kaitaraiwa takitahi me ngā waka i waenganui i ngā pūwāhi e rua me ngā pāhekoheko a tētahi ki tētahi.
reference: Traffic Flow is the study of the movement of individual drivers and vehicles between two points and the interactions they make with one another.

source: E pōuri ana, he uaua te rangahau i te rere o ngā waka nā te mea e kore e taea te matapae i te whanonga taraiwa ki te mōhiotanga kotahi-rau ōrau.
reference: Unfortunately, studying traffic flow is difficult because driver behavior cannot be predicted with one-hundred percent certainty.

source: Maringanui, kei roto i te whānui āhua ū ngā whanonga noa o ngā kaitaraiwa; nō reira, ka āhua ōrite ngā ia waka, ā, ka taea te whakaata āwhiwhi ā-pāngarau.
reference: Fortunately, drivers tend to behave within a reasonably consistent range; thus, traffic streams tend to have some reasonable consistency and can be roughly represented mathematically.

source: Kia pai ake te whakaatu i te rerenga waka, kua whakatūria ngā hononga i waenganui i ngā āhuatanga matua e toru: (1) te rere, (2) te apiapi, ā, (3) te tere.
reference: To better represent traffic flow, relationships have been established between the three main characteristics: (1) flow, (2) density, and (3) velocity.

source: He mea āwhina ēnei hononga ki te whakamahere, te hoahoa, me te mahi i ngā hanganga taha huarahi.
reference: These relationships help in planning, design, and operations of roadway facilities.

source: Ko ngārara ngā kararehe tuatahi i whai hā. Ko ō rātou pukenga ki te rere i māmā ake ai te ārai atu i ngā hoariri, ki te kai kai, ki te kimi hoa hoki.
reference: Insects were the first animals to take to the air. Their ability to fly helped them evade enemies more easily and find food and mates more efficiently.

source: Kei te nuinga o ngā pepeke te huanga o te āheinga ki te kōnumi i ō rātou parirau ki te tinana.
reference: Most insects have the advantage of being able to fold their wings back along the body.

source: Mā korā ka whānui ake ngā wāhi iti hei huna i ngā konihi.
reference: This gives them a wider range of small places to hide from predators.

source: I tēnei rā, ko ngā pepeke e kore e taea te kōnumi i ō rātou parirau ko ngā uruururoroa, ko ngā piriwai anake.
reference: Today, the only insects that cannot fold back their wings are dragon flies and mayflies.

source: I ngā mano tau i mua, ko te tangata e kīa nei ko Aristarchus i kī ake ko te whānau a Tamanui te rā i huri rauna i te rā.
reference: Thousands of years ago, a man called Aristarchus said that the Solar System moved around the Sun.

source: I whakapono ētahi tāngata e tika ana ia engari ka rerekē te whakapono a ētahi; arā kia huri haere te Whānau a Tamanuiterā i te Ao, tae atu ki te Rā (tae rā anō ki ērā atu whetū).
reference: Some people thought he was right but many people believed the opposite; that the Solar System moved around the Earth, including the Sun (and even the other stars).

source: He whaitake te āhua, nā te mea kāore i te rangona te neke haeretanga o te ao, nē?
reference: This seems sensible, because the Earth doesn't feel as if it's moving, does it?

source: Ko te awa Amazon te awa nunui, roroa tuarua o te ao. Neke atu i te 8 reanga wai e pūritia ana e ia.
reference: The Amazon River is the second longest and the biggest river on Earth. It carries more than 8 times as much water as the second biggest river.

source: Ko te Amazon te awa whānui o Papatūānuku nei, e ono maero te whānuitanga.
reference: The Amazon is also the widest river on Earth, at times six miles wide.

source: Kei te ahu mai te nui o te 20 ōrau o te wai e rere ana i ngā awa o te ao ki ngā moana i te Amazon.
reference: A full 20 percent of the water that pours out of the planet's rivers into the oceans comes from the Amazon.

source: Ko te roa o te Amazon River matua ko te 6,387 km (3,980 maero). Kei te kohi ia i te wai mai i ngā tini mano awa iti iho.
reference: The main Amazon River is 6,387 km (3,980 miles). It collects water from thousands of smaller rivers.

source: Ahakoa i haere tonu te hanga-koeko ki te kohatu tae atu ki te mutunga o te Old Kingdom, kore rawa i pahika ngā koeko o Giza i te rahi me te kounga hangarau o te hanganga.
reference: Although pyramid-building in stone continued until the end of the Old Kingdom, the pyramids of Giza were never surpassed in their size and the technical excellence of their construction.

source: I mīharo ngā tāngata Īhipa tawhito New Kingtom ki ngā tohu whakamaumahara o ō rātou tūpuna, i taua wā ka nui ake i te kotahi mano tau te tawhito.
reference: New Kingdom ancient Egyptians marvelled at their predecessors monuments, which were then well over a thousand year old.

source: Tata ki te 800 te taupori o Vatican City. Koirā te whenua motuhake iti rawa o te ao, waihoki ko te whenua he iti rawa te taupori.
reference: Vatican City's population is around 800. It is the smallest independent country in the world and the country with the lowest population.

source: Ko te reo ka whakamahia e te Whatikana ki roto i ōna ture me ōna pārongorongo ko te reo Itāria.
reference: Vatican City uses Italian in its legislation and official communications.

source: Ko te reo Itāria hoki te reo o ia rā e whakamahia ana e te nuinga o rātou e mahi ana ki te takiwā, ā, kei te tino whakamahia te reo Rātini ki ngā kawa ā-hāhi.
reference: Italian is also the everyday language used by most of those who work in the state while Latin is often used in religious ceremonies.

source: Ko ngā kainoho katoa o Vatican City he Rōmana Katorika.
reference: All citizens of Vatican City are Roman Catholic.

source: Kua mōhio te tangata ki ngā pūmotu matū pūtake pērā i te koura, te hiriwa, te kapa mai rā anō, nā te mea ka taea te kite i te katoa o ēnei ki te taiao ki te āhuahanga tūturu, ā, he āhua māmā te maina ki ngā taputapu nō nehe.
reference: People have known about basic chemical elements such as gold, silver, and copper from antiquity, as these can all be discovered in nature in native form and are relatively simple to mine with primitive tools.

source: Aristotle, te tangata whakaaro hohonu, i whakapae kua hanga ngā mea katoa i roto i ngā ranunga o tētahi o ngā pūmotu e whā. Ko te one, ko te wai, ko te hau me te ahi.
reference: Aristotle, a philosopher, theorised that everything is made up of a mixture of one or more of four elements. They were earth, water, air, and fire.

source: Ka pērā anō tēnei i ngā āhuatanga e whā o te matū (ki te raupapa ōrite): arā te totoka, te wē, te hau, te wētoto, ahakoa kua puta te ariā i a ia ka panoni ēnei ki ngā matū hou hei hanga i ngā mea e kitea ana e tātou.
reference: This was more like the four states of matter (in the same order): solid, liquid, gas, and plasma, though he also theorised that they change into new substances to form what we see.

source: Ko ngā koranu ko te ranunga noa o ngā mētara e rua, neke atu rānei. Kauaka e wareware he nui ngā pūmotu ki te taka pūmotu
reference: Alloys are basically a mixture of two or more metals. Don't forget that there are many elements on the periodic table.

source: Ko ngā matū pērā i te konumohe me te konupūma e whai whakaarotia ana he mētara. Āe mārika, he mētara atu anō pēnei i te hiriwa me te koura.
reference: Elements like calcium and potassium are considered metals. Of course, there are also metals like silver and gold.

source: Ka taea ngā kōranu e mau kōranu-kore ana mō ētahi mātū, pērā ki te waro.
reference: You can also have alloys that include small amounts of non-metallic elements like carbon.

source: Ngā mea katoa o te ao nei kua hangaia ki te matū. Ko ngā matū katoa kua hangaia ki ngā mea iti nei e kīa nei ko te ngota.
reference: Everything in the Universe is made of matter. All matter is made of tiny particles called atoms.

source: He pērā rawa te tino riki rawa o ngā ngota ka taea e ngā tini tiriona ngota te noho ki roto i te irakati ki te mutunga o tēnei rerenga.
reference: Atoms are so incredibly tiny that trillions of them could fit into the period at the end of this sentence.

source: Nā tēnā, i whakahoahoa ai te penerakau ki te tini tangata i te wā i hangaia mai.
reference: Thus, the pencil was a good friend to many people when it came out.

source: Te pōuri hoki, kua iti ake te mana me te whakamahinga o te pene rākau i te mea kua ara ake ētahi tikanga tuhituhi hou.
reference: Sadly, as newer methods of writing have emerged, the pencil has been relegated to lesser status and uses.

source: Ka tuhia ngā karere ki runga rorohiko ināianei, hei aha noa iho te tāonga whakakoi
reference: People now write messages on computer screens, never having to come close to a sharpener.

source: Ka taea anake te whai whakaaro ake ka ahatia ki te papa pātuhi inā ka puta mai tētahi mea hou.
reference: One can only wonder what the keyboard will become when something newer comes along.

source: Ko te tikanga o te pahū fission he hari i te ngoi hei whakaemi i nukara he tini ngā iraoho me ngā iramoe.
reference: The fission bomb works on the principle that it takes energy to put together a nucleus with many protons and neutrons.

source: E takahurihuri ana pēnei i ngā kāta tumaha mā runga i te puke. E whakawehe nei anō i te poka pū, ka tukuna ai ētahi o taua ngoi.
reference: Sort of like rolling a heavy cart up a hill. Splitting the nucleus up again then releases some of that energy.

source: He karihi pāhekeheke kei ētahi ngota, nā korā ka whakawehi ai ahakoa kāore he tuke, he iti rānei te tuke.
reference: Some atoms have unstable nuclei which means that they tend to break apart with little or no nudging.

source: Kua hangaia te mata o te Marama ki ngā kōhatu me te puehu. E kīia ana ko te papa ā-waho o te Marama ko te kirimarama.
reference: The surface of the Moon is made of rocks and dust. The outer layer of the Moon is called the crust.

source: He tata ki te 70 km te mātotoru o te kirinuku ki te taha tata, ā, e 100 km te mātotoru ki te taha tawhiti.
reference: The crust is about 70 km thick on the near side and 100 km thick on the far side.

source: Ko raro i te maria he tūpuhi, ki tuawhenua he whānui ake.
reference: It is thinner under the maria and thicker under the highlands.

source: Ka nui ake pea te maria ki te taha tata i te mea he kōrahirahi ake te kirinuku. He māmā ake kia whanake ake te tahepuia ki te mata.
reference: There may be more maria on the near side because the crust is thinner. It was easier for lava to rise up to the surface.

source: Kei te ngako o ngā ariā kiko ko te rapu i ngā mea e tino whakaoho ana i te tangata, e pai ana ki a rātou rānei.
reference: Content theories are centered on finding what makes people tick or appeals to them.

source: Kei te marohi ēnei ariā kei ētahi tāngata he hiahia, he pīrangi hoki/rānei kua whakatōngia ki roto i a rātou e pakeke haere ana ki te pakeketanga.
reference: These theories suggest that people have certain needs and/or desires which have been internalized as they mature to adulthood.

source: Kei te aro atu ēnei ariā ki ngā āhuatanga o ētahi tāngata e tuku ana i a rātou ki te hiahia i ngā mea e hiahiatia ana e rātou, ā, he aha ngā mea ki tō rātou taiao ka whakarite i a rātou ki te mahi, ki te kore mahi rānei i ētahi mahi.
reference: These theories look at what it is about certain people that make them want the things that they do and what things in their environment will make them do or not do certain things.

source: Ko ngā ariā kiko rorotu e rua ko te Hierarchy of Needs Theory a Maslow, me te Two Factor Theory a Hertzberg.
reference: Two popular content theories are Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs Theory and Hertzberg's Two Factor Theory.

source: I te nuinga o te wā, e rua ngā whanonga ka puta mai pea i te tīmatanga o ngā kaiwhakahaere te ārahi i ō rātou hoamahi o mua. Ki tētahi pito ko te ngana ki te noho tonu hei "tētahi o ngā autaia" (ngā kō rānei).
reference: Generally speaking, two behaviors can emerge as managers begin to lead their former peers. One end of the spectrum is trying to remain “one of the guys” (or gals).

source: He uaua ki tēnei momo kaiwhakahaere te whakatau i ngā whakatau kāore e rataina, te whakamahi i ngā mahi whakatika, te arotake mahi, te tuku haepapa, me te whakarite kia noho haepapa ngā tāngata.
reference: This type of manager has difficulty making unpopular decisions, performing disciplinary action, performance evaluations, assigning responsibility, and holding people accountable.

source: Ki tērā atu pito o te whānuitanga, ka huri tētahi ki tētahi takitahi kāore e mōhiotia ana ki a ia me panoni ia i ngā mahi katoa kua mahia e te rōpū kia noho ko tōna ake.
reference: At the other end of the spectrum, one morphs into an unrecognizable individual that feels he or she must change everything the team has been doing and make it their own.

source: Waihoki, kei te noho haepapa te kaiārahi mō te eke me te hinga o te kapa.
reference: After all, the leader is ultimately responsible for the success and failure of the team.

source: He nui ngā wā ka puta i tēnei whanonga ngā wehenga i waenganui i ngā kaiārahi me te toenga o te rōpū.
reference: This behavior oftentimes results in rifts between the leaders and the rest of the team.

source: E mau nei te taumata kōunga mō ngā roopū mariko ki ngā tīma taketake, heoi he rerekētanga iti nei.
reference: Virtual teams are held to the same standards of excellence as conventional teams, but there are subtle differences.

source: I ētahi wā ka noho ngā mema tīma mariko hei āhuatanga whakapākanga mō tō rātou rōpū ā-kiko tata.
reference: Virtual team members often function as the point of contact for their immediate physical group.

source: I te nuinga o te wā he nui ake tō rātou tino rangatiratanga tēnā i ngā mema o ngā tīma noa, i te mea ko ā rātou tīma ka hui kei te āhua o ngā rohe wā rerekē ka kore pea e māramatia e tā rātou kaiwhakahaere ā-rohe.
reference: They often have more autonomy than conventional team members as their teams may meet according to varying time zones which may not be understood by their local management.

source: Ko te noho mai o tētahi "kapa kāore i te kitea" tūturu (Larson and LaFasto, 1989, p109) hoki tētahi wāhanga ahurei o tētahi kapa mariko.
reference: The presence of a true “invisible team” (Larson and LaFasto, 1989, p109) is also a unique component of a virtual team.

source: Ko te "tīma kore kitea" te tīma whakahaere e noho ai hei rīpoatatanga mā ngā mema. Ka whakatū te tīma kore kitea ngā taumata mō ia mema.
reference: The “invisible team” is the management team to which each of the members report. The invisible team sets the standards for each member.

source: He aha i hiahia ai tētahi umanga te mahi i tētahi hātepe whakapau wā ki te whakarite i tētahi umanga whakaako? Ko tētahi whāinga mō te whakamahi i ngā kaupapa ako ki roto i tētahi parakatihi auaha.
reference: Why would an organization want to go through the time consuming process of establishing a learning organization? One goal for putting organizational learning concepts into practice is innovation.

source: I te wā he pai te whakamahi i ngā rawa katoa e wātea ana whakawhiti atu i ngā tari mahi o tētahi whakahaere, ka ara ake pea te auaha me te atamai.
reference: When all available resources are effectively used across the functional departments of an organization, creativity and ingenuity can transpire.

source: Me te aha, ko te hātepe o tētahi whakahaere e mahi tahi ana kia eke panuku i tētahi ārai e taea ai te arataki ki tētahi hātepe auaha hou hei whakarato i tā te kiritaki hiahia.
reference: As a result, the process of an organization working together to overcome an obstacle can lead to a new innovative process to serve the customer's need.

source: E maromahue ai tētahi hinonga, me whakatōkia e ōna rangatira tētahi momo wairua maromahue, te tuari mātauranga, me tēnei mea te ako hoki.
reference: Before an organization can be innovative, leadership must create a culture of innovation as well as shared knowledge and organizational learning.

source: E whakamārama ana a Angel (2006) i te huarahi Kore-otinga hei tikanga ārahi i ngā rōpū whakahaere kia eke ai rātou ki ngā taumata e whāia ana.
reference: Angel (2006), explains the Continuum approach as a method being used to help organizations reach a higher level of performance.

source: Kei ngā raraunga koiora-ā-io ngā whakaaturanga ā-kiko mō tētahi aronga ā-ariā ki te mātai i te whakaaroaro. Nā reira māna te kaupapa rangahau e whakawhāiti, ā, ka pū ake hoki te aronga.
reference: Neurobiological data provide physical evidence for a theoretical approach to the investigation of cognition. Therefore it narrows the research area and makes it much more exact.

source: Kei te tautoko te hāngaitanga i waenganui i te pānga hinengaro me te whanonga i ngā kaipūtaiao ki ā rātou rangahau.
reference: The correlation between brain pathology and behaviour supports scientists in their research.

source: Kua roa e mōhiotia ana mā ngā momo rerekē o ā te roro mate, whara, panoni whanokē, pukupuku anō hoki e pā ki te whanonga, ā, ka hanga panoni hoki ki ētahi mahi ā-hinengaro.
reference: It has been known for a long time that different types of brain damage, traumas, lesions, and tumours affect behaviour and cause changes in some mental functions.

source: Ko te pikinga ake o ngā hangarau hou e tuku ana i a tātou ki te kite, ki te mātai i ngā hanganga me ngā hātepe roro kāore anō kia kitea.
reference: The rise of new technologies allows us to see and investigate brain structures and processes never seen before.

source: Ka homai tēnei i ngā tini pārongo me ngā rauemi hei hanga i ngā tauira whakaata e āwhina ai i a tātou kia whai māramatanga mō ngā hātepe o roto i ō tātou hinengaro.
reference: This provides us with a lot of information and material to build simulation models which help us to understand processes in our mind.

source: Ahakoa ko te AI e hāngai pū ana ki ngā pakiwaitara pūtaiao, he mea nui te AI ki te ao rorohiko, ko te whakamātau i ngā whanonga, ko te ako me te urutau i te taumata whakaaro ki tētahi mīhini.
reference: Although AI has a strong connotation of science fiction, AI forms a very important branch of computer science, dealing with behavior, learning and intelligent adaptation in a machine.

source: Kei roto i te rangahau atamai horihori te hanga mīhini kia whakangāwari i ngā mahi e hiahia ana ki te whanonga atamai.
reference: Research in AI involves making machines to automate tasks that require intelligent behavior.

source: Ko ētahi tauira ko te whakahaere, te whakamahere me te whakarite wā, te āheinga ki te whakahoki i ngā whakatau me ngā pātai a ngā kiritaki, tae atu hoki ki te mōhiotanga o te tuhi ā-ringa, te reo me te kanohi.
reference: Examples include control, planning and scheduling, the ability to answer customer diagnoses and questions, as well as handwriting recognition, voice and face.

source: Kua rerekē haere ēnei tūmomo āhuatanga, ko te aronga nui hoki he kimi rongoā mō nga mate tuturu nei.
reference: Such things have become separate disciplines, which focus on providing solutions to real life problems.

source: Kei te whakamahia nuitia te pūnaha Atamai Horihori ki ngā mātaitanga o te ohaoha, te rongoā, te kaipūkaha, te tauā hoki, pērā i te hanganga ki ētahi taupānga pūmanawa rorohiko ā-kāinga, kēmu ataata hoki.
reference: The AI ​​system is now often used in the fields of economics, medicine, engineering and the military, as has been built in several home computer and video game software applications.

source: He wāhanga nui te haerenga o te akomanga. I ētahi wā ka tino hiahia te kaiako kia haere ōna ākonga ki tōna taha ki ngā wāhi e kore e taea mā runga pahi.
reference: Field trips are a large part of any classroom. Quite often a teacher would love to take her students places to which a bus trip is not an option.

source: Ko te huarahi a te ao matihiko ko te whakahaere haerenga ā-tuihono nei. Ka kitea e ngā tauira i ngā pātaka pupuri tāonga, te haere ki te tirotiro ki te puna moana, he whakanui ake rānei i te ātaahuatanga toi i ā rātou e noho ana ki te karaehe.
reference: Technology offers the solution with virtual field trips. Students can look at museum artifacts, visit an aquarium, or admire beautiful art while sitting with their class.

source: He ara pai te haere tahi ā-mariko i tētahi haerenga hei whakaatu whakaaro mō te haerenga, me te kōrero mō ngā wheako ki ngā akomanga e haere ake nei.
reference: Sharing a field trip virtually is also a great way to reflect a on a trip and share experiences with future classes.

source: Hei tauira, ia tau ngā ākonga mai i te kura o Peneti i te raki o Karorina ka hanga i tētahi whārangi ipurangi mō tā rātou haerenga ki te Tāte Matua, ia tau ka whakahoungia te whārangi ipurangi, heoi ko ngā whārangi o mua ka waiho ki te ipurangi hei tuhinga hukihuki.
reference: For example, each year students from Bennet School in North Carolina design a website about their trip to the State Capital, each year the website gets remodeled, but old versions are kept online to serve as a scrapbook.

source: He pai ngā rangitaki ki te whakakoi ake i ngā pukenga tuhi. Ahakoa ngā hapa, ka rerekē haere tēnei āhuatanga i te hunga pānui.
reference: Blogs can also help improve student writing. While students often begin their blog experience with sloppy grammar and spelling, the presence of an audience generally changes that.

source: Nā te mea he nui te wā ko ngā ākonga te minenga tino whakahē, ka tīmata te kaituhi rangitaki ki te whai kia whakapai ake i te tuhinga hei karo i te whakahēnga.
reference: Since students are often the most critical audience, the blog writer begins to strive to improve writing to avoid criticism.

source: Mā te rangitaki hoki "e tuku ai ngā ākonga te mōhio ake mō te ao e noho nei rātou." Ka whakahihikotia ngā ākonga kia koi, kia manawa reka, i runga i te hiahia kia whāngai i te aronga mai o te minenga (Toto, 2004).
reference: Also blogging "forces students to become more savvy about the world around them." The need to feed the interest of the audience inspires students to be clever and interesting (Toto, 2004).

source: He rauemi te rangitaki e whakaaweawe ai i te mahi tahi, me te akiaki i ngā ākonga kia whāroa ake te ako i tua atu i tētahi rangi noa i te kura.
reference: Blogging is a tool that inspires collaboration, and encourages students to extend learning well beyond the traditional school day.

source: Mā te tika o te mahi rangitaki "e hihiko ai ngā tauira kia mōhio ki te aromātai, ki te arohaehae; ahakoa e aro kē ana ki ngā āhuatanga ipurangi, ka mōhio ngā tauira kei hea ō rātou whakaaro e haere ana mō te āhua ki ngā tuhinga a ētahi atu, me te waihanga hoki i ō rātou ake tirohanga ki ngā take nei" (Oravec, 2002).
reference: Appropriate use of blogs "can empower students to become more analytical and critical; through actively responding to Internet materials, students can define their positions in the context of others' writings as well as outline their own perspectives on particular issues (Oravec, 2002).

source: Ko te tāone matua papai rawa e reorua ana, a Ottowa e whakaatua mai ana i ngā whare tapere toi, ngā whare pupuri tāonga me ngā āhutanga o ōnamata tae noa ki ērā o anamata.
reference: Ottawa is Canada's charming, bilingual capital and features an array of art galleries and museums that showcase Canada's past and present.

source: Neke atu ki te tonga ko Niagara Falls, ā, ko te raki te kāinga mō te rerehua māori kāore i te tino mōhiotia o te Muskoka, ki tua atu anō hoki.
reference: Farther south is Niagara Falls and the north is home to the untapped natural beauty of the Muskoka and beyond.

source: Hei tā te hunga o waho, katoa ēnei mea e tohu ana ko Ontario he rohe ātaahua rawa atu ki Kānata.
reference: All these things and more highlight Ontario as what is considered quintessentially Canadian by outsiders.

source: Ko ngā wāhi nui kei te raki e kōpā ana te noho a te tangata, engari anō ngā wāhi rō ngahere, kāore nei e tino pērā rawa.
reference: Large areas further north are quite sparsely populated and some is nearly uninhabited wilderness.

source: Mō tētahi whakatauritenga o te taupori ka whakaohorere i te tokomaha: He nui ake ngā African Americans e noho ana ki Ngā Whenua Tōpū o Amerika i ngā kirirarau nō Kānata.
reference: For a comparison of population that surprises many: There are more African Americans living in the US than there are Canadian citizens.

source: Kei te Indian Ocean ngā motu o Aherika ki te Tonga, kei tua mai o te takutai ki te rāwhiti o Awherika.
reference: The East African Islands are in the Indian Ocean off the eastern coast of Africa.

source: Ko Marakāhia te mea nui rawa, ā, he paparahi ki tōna kotahi e hāngai ana ki ngā kararehe mohoao.
reference: Madagascar is by far the biggest, and a continent on its own when it comes to wildlife.

source: He whenua motuhake te nuinga o ngā motu iti iho, he hononga ki Wīwī rānei, ā, kei te mōhiotia hei wāhi whakatā taha moana hāneanea rawa.
reference: Most of the smaller islands are independent nations, or associated with France, and known as luxury beach resorts.

source: Nā ngā Arapi ngā Ihirama i heri mai ki ēnei whenua, ā, he nui te āhua i ahu atu ki ngā Comoro me ngā Mayotte.
reference: The Arabs also brought Islam to the lands, and it took in a big way in the Comoros and Mayotte.

source: I tīmata te pānga me te mahi koroniara Ūropi i te rautau 15, i te rapunga o te kaihōpara nō Potukara a Vasco da Gama i te Cape Route mai i Ūropi ki Īnia.
reference: European influence and colonialism began in the 15th century, as Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama found the Cape Route from Europe to India.

source: Kei te raki ka noho te Sahel ki te taha o te takiwā, ā, ki te tonga, ki te hauāuru hoki te Atlantic Ocean.
reference: In the north the region is bounded by the Sahel, and in the south and west by the Atlantic Ocean.

source: Wāhine: E tautuhia ana kia kīia e te wāhine hāereere kua mārena rātou, ahakoa kua mārena, kāore rānei.
reference: Women: It is recommended that any women travellers say that they are married, regardless of actual marital status.

source: He mea tautoko te mau hoki i tētahi ringi (engari kia kaua tētahi he nui te utu o tōna āhua.
reference: It is helpful to also wear a ring (just not one that looks too expensive.

source: Me mōhio te wahine he rerekētanga ahurea ka ahu atu pea ki ngā mahi e whai whakaarotia ana e rātou he whakatīwheta, ā, ka whāia, ka mauria pea te ringa, aha atu.
reference: Women should realize that cultural differences may result in what they would consider harassment and it is not uncommon to be followed, grabbed by the arm, etc.

source: Kia kaha ki te whakakahore i te tāne, ā, kaua e mataku ki te tū pakari ai (ahakoa ngā rerekētanga ahurea, kāore tonu i te pai!).
reference: Be firm in turning down men, and don't be afraid to stand your ground (cultural differences or not, it doesn't make it ok!).

source: I whakatūria te tāone nui hou o Casablanca e ngā kaihī ika Berber i te rau tau tuatekau BCE, ā, i whakamahia e te hunga Phoenician, Rōmana, Merenid anō hoki hei tētahi wāpu rautaki ko Anfa te ingoa.
reference: The modern city of Casablanca was founded by Berber fishermen in the 10th century BCE, and was used by the Phoenicians, Romans, and the Merenids as a strategic port called Anfa.

source: Nā te iwi Potugese i turaki, ka hanga mai anō me te ingoa o Casa Branca, ka whakarerea hoki whai muri mai i te rū whenua i te tau 1755.
reference: The Portuguese destroyed it and rebuilt it under the name Casa Branca, only to abandon it after an earthquake in 1755.

source: I hangaia anō e te sultan o Marako te tāone nui hei Daru I-Badya, ā, i tapaina ki te ingoa o Casablanca e ngā kaihokohoko Pāniora nā rātou he wāhi hokohoko i whakatū ki reira.
reference: The Moroccan sultan rebuilt the city as Daru l-Badya and it was given the name Casablanca by Spanish traders who established trading bases there.

source: Ko Casablanca tētahi o ngā wāhi iti rawa te ngahau hei hokohoko ki te katoa o Marako.
reference: Casablanca is one of the least interesting places to shop in all of Morocco.

source: Huri noa i old Medina he māmā te rapu wāhi e hoko ana i ngā rawa tuku iho Māroko, pērā i te tagine, i te uku, i ngā rawa kirikau, i ngā hookah, i te whānuitanga o ngā geegaws, engari mā te tūruhi te katoa.
reference: Around the old Medina it's easy to find places selling traditional Moroccan goods, such as tagines, pottery, leather goods, hookahs, and a whole spectrum of geegaws, but it's all for the tourists.

source: He tāone tūruhi a Goma o te Democratic Republic of Congo i te rāwhiti rā anō tata ki Rwanda.
reference: Goma is a tourist city of the Democratic Republic of Congo in the extreme east near Rwanda.

source: I te tau 2002 i horo a Goma i te tahepuia mai i te maunga puia o Nyiragongo, ā, nā tērā i tanu te nuinga o ngā tiriti o te tāone, inarā te pokapū o te tāone.
reference: In 2002 Goma was destroyed by lava from the Nyiragongo volcano which buried most of the town’s streets, particularly the town centre.

source: Ahakoa he āhua haumaru a Goma, me rangahau ngā toronga kei waho i Goma hei mārama ki te āhua o te whawhai e haere tonu ana ki te takiwā North Kivu.
reference: While Goma is reasonably safe, any visits outside of Goma should be researched to understand the state of the fighting that persists in the North Kivu province.

source: Ko te tāone te pūtake hoki hei piki i te rangitoto, i a Nyiragongo, kei reira hoki ētahi o ngā whaiwhai maunga korira iti te utu o Āwherika.
reference: The city is also the base to climb the Nyiragongo volcano along with some of the cheapest Mountain Gorilla tracking in Africa.

source: Ka taea e koe te eke i ngā boda-boda (tākihi motopāika) hei huri rāuna i a Goma. Ko te utu noa (ki reira) he ~500 Congolese Francs mō te haerenga iti.
reference: You can use boda-boda (motorcycle taxi) to get around Goma. The normal (local) price is ~500 Congolese Francs for the short ride.

source: Hui tahi ki tōna āhua kore āhei atu, kua whakamahia a "Timbuktu" hei kupu whakarite mō te whenua tauhou, tawhiti hoki.
reference: Combined with its relative inaccessibility, "Timbuktu" has come to be used as a metaphor for exotic, distant lands.

source: I tēnei wā, he tāone tino pōhara a Timbuktu, ahakoa he whakamaneatanga tūruhi i runga i tōna rongo, ā, he tauranga waka rererangi ki reira.
reference: Today, Timbuktu is an impoverished town, although its reputation makes it a tourist attraction, and it has an airport.

source: I te tau 1990, i tāpiritia ia ki te rārangi o ngā wāhi world heritage e mōrearea ana, i runga i te whakatuma o ngā oneone koraha.
reference: In 1990, it was added to the list of world heritage sites in danger, due to the threat of desert sands.

source: Koirā tētahi o ngā tūnga matua i te wā o te PBS special Wonders of the African World o Henry Louis Gates.
reference: It was one of the major stops during Henry Louis Gates' PBS special Wonders of the African World.

source: He tīno rerekē te tāone i te toinga atu o ngā tāone o te whenua, i te mea he tāera Arabi tēnā i te Awherika.
reference: The city is in stark contrast to the rest of the country's cities, because it has more of an Arabic flair than of an African.

source: Kei te raki mā rāwhiti o Āwherika ki te Tonga te Kruger National Park (KNP), ā, kei te pae o Mozambique ki te rāwhiti, ki Zimbabwe ki te raki, ā, ko te pae tonga ko te Crocodile River.
reference: The Kruger National Park (KNP) lies in the north-east of South Africa and runs along the border of Mozambique in the east, Zimbabwe in the north, and the southern border is the Crocodile River.

source: Kei te hōkai 19,500 km₂ te rahi o te pāka, ā, kei te whakawehea ki ngā wāhi rauropi 14 rerekē, kei te tautīnei ia wāhi i ngā karerehe rerekē.
reference: The park covers 19,500 km² and is divided in 14 different ecozones, each supporting different wildlife.

source: Koirā tētahi o ngā mea whakamanea matua o Awherika ki te Tonga, ā, e whai whakaarohia ana ia hei tohu matua o ngā South African National Parks (SANParks).
reference: It is one of the main attractions of South Africa and it is considered the flagship of South African National Parks (SANParks).

source: Mō ngā Rohenga Wao Nui o Aherika ki te Tonga, he utu e hāngai ana i te urunga ki ngā rohenga.
reference: As with all South African National Parks, there are daily conservation and entry fees for the park.

source: He whaihua pea kia hokona tētahi Wild Card, māna e tuku te kuhunga atu ki ētahi kōwhiringa o ngā pāka ki Āwherika ki te Tonga, ki te katoa rānei o ngā South African National Parks.
reference: It may also be beneficial for one to buy a Wild Card, which provides entry to either selections of parks in South Africa or all of the South African National Parks.

source: Nā Hong Kong Island te ingoa o te rohe o Hong Kong, ā, koirā te wāhi ka whakaarohia e ngā tini tūruhi hei aronga matua.
reference: Hong Kong Island gives the territory of Hong Kong its name and is the place that many tourists regard as the main focus.

source: Kua kōrerotia te tini o ngā whare nunui e hanga ai i te huapae o Hong Kong anō nei ko te pae mahere pīataata e mōhio whānuitia ana e ngā wai o Victoria Harbour.
reference: The parade of buildings that make the Hong Kong skyline has been likened to a glittering bar chart that is made apparent by the presence of the waters of Victoria Harbour.

source: Ki te kite i ngā tirohanga rawe rawa atu o Hong Kong, me wehe i te motu ka haere ai ki te tāhuna Kowloon kei tērā atu taha.
reference: To get the best views of Hong Kong, leave the island and head for the Kowloon waterfront opposite.

source: Kei te kiato te nuinga o te whanaketanga ā-tāone o Hong Kong Island ki te whenua taumanu ki te taha o te tai raki.
reference: The great majority of Hong Kong Island's urban development is densely packed on reclaimed land along the northern shore.

source: Koinei te wāhi i tangohia e te hunga koroniara nō Peretānia mō rātou anō, nō reira ki te rapu koe i te taunakitanga o te hītori koroniara o te takiwā, koinei tētahi wāhi pai hei tīmata.
reference: This is the place the British colonisers took as their own and so if you are looking for evidence of the territory's colonial past, this is a good place to start.

source: Ko ngā Sundarbans ngā tātua ritora mangrove nui rawa i te ao, e toro ana i te 80 km (50 mairo) ki roto ki te Bangladeshi me te Iniana whenuaroto tae atu ki te takutai.
reference: The Sundarbans are the largest littoral mangrove belt in the world, stretching 80 km (50 mi) into the Bangladeshi and Indian hinterland from the coast.

source: I ehakatauhia ko te Sundarbans he UNESCO World Heritage Site. Ko te ingoa o te wāhanga ngahere kei te rohe Inia, ko Sundarbans National Park.
reference: The Sundarbans has been declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The part of the forest within Indian territory is called Sundarbans National Park.

source: Ehara noa ngā ngahengahe nei i te repo waikure - kei roto ētahi o ngā tūnga whakamutunga o te ngahengahe nunui i kapi ai i te raorao Gangetic.
reference: The forests aren't just mangrove swamps though — they include some of the last remaining stands of the mighty jungles which once covered the Gangetic plain.

source: Kei te horahanga o te 3,850 km2 ngā Sundarbans, ā, ko te kotahi-hautoru e whakakapia ana ki ngā wāhi wai/kōreporepo.
reference: The Sundarbans cover an area of 3,850 km², of which about one-third is covered in water/marsh areas.

source: Mai i te 1966 kua noho iho te Sundarbans hei whakamaurutanga mohoao, ā, e ai ki te kōrero he 400 ngā tāika ariki Bengal me ngā tia korotiwhatiwha e 30,000 i roto i te wāhi.
reference: Since 1966 the Sundarbans have been a wildlife sanctuary, and it is estimated that there are now 400 Royal Bengal tigers and about 30,000 spotted deer in the area.

source: Ka wehe ngā pahi i te teihana kei te pokapū (kei tērā atu taha o te awa) i te wā e awatea ana, ahakoa ko te nuinga, mātua rā, ko ērā e haere whaka te raki ana, tae atu anō hoki ki a Jakar/Bumthang ka wehe mai i te 6.30 ki te 7.30.
reference: Buses depart the inter-district bus station (across the river) throughout the day, though most, especially those heading to the east and Jakar/Bumthang leave between 06:30 and 07:30.

source: I runga i te noho kī o ngā pahi waenga-rohe i te nuinga o ngā wā, he rautaki pai te hoko tīkiti ētahi rā i mua.
reference: As the inter-district buses are often full, it is advisable to purchase a ticket a few days in advance.

source: Kei te nuinga o ngā rohe ko ngā Japanese Coaster Buses paku, he āhuru, he pakari anō hoki.
reference: Most districts are served by small Japanese Coaster Buses, which are comfortable and sturdy.

source: He tere ake, he hāneanea ake te tuari tēkehi ki te haereere ki ngā wahi e tata ana, pērā ki a Paro (Nu 150) me Punkha (Nu 200).
reference: Shared taxis are a quick and comfortable means to travel to nearby places, such as Paro (Nu 150) and Punakha (Nu 200).

source: He piriti here-taura te Oyapock River Bridge. Ka whakawhiti atu i te Oyapock River hei tūhono i ngā tāone nui o Oiapoque ki Parīhi, me Saint-Georges de l'Oyapock ki French Guiana.
reference: The Oyapock River Bridge is a cable-stayed bridge. It spans the Oyapock River to link the cities of Oiapoque in Brazil and Saint-Georges de l'Oyapock in French Guiana.

source: Ka piki te teitei o ngā pourewa e rua ki te 83 mita, ā, e 378 mita te roa, e rua ngā ara o te 3.50 m te whānui.
reference: The two towers rise to a height of 83 meters, it's 378 meters long and it has two lanes of 3.50 m wide.

source: He 15 mita te poutū māhea o raro iho i te piriti. I oti te hanganga i te Ākuhata 2011, kāore i whakatuwhera mō ngā waka tae rawa ki te marama o Māehe i te tau 2017.
reference: The vertical clearance under the bridge is 15 meters. Construction was completed in August 2011, it didn't open to traffic until March 2017.

source: Kua whakaritea kia pai katoa ngā mahi a te piriti hei te Mahuru o te tau 2017, hei taua wā kua oti pea ngā wāhi tirotiro hei ārai o Parihi.
reference: The bridge is scheduled to be fully operational in September 2017, when the Brazilian customs checkpoints are expected to be finished.

source: Ko te Guarani te iwi taketake hira rawa e noho ana ki te whenua e kīia ana ko Eastern Paraguay ināianei, i te noho hei kaiaruaru āhua-tipiwhenua, i te mahi hoki i te ahuwhenua mā rātou anō.
reference: The Guaraní were the most significant indigenous group inhabiting what is now Eastern Paraguay, living as semi-nomadic hunters who also practised subsistence agriculture.

source: I nōhia ai te rohe Chaco e ngā iwi taketake pērā ki ngā Guaycur'u me ngā Payagu'a, i ora ai rātou i te whai kararehe me te hī ika.
reference: The Chaco region was home to other groups of indigenous tribes such as the Guaycurú and Payaguá, who survived by hunting, gathering and fishing.

source: I te rautau 16, i whānau mai a Paraguay, ko tōna ingoa kārangaranga hoki ko "Te Rohe nui o ngā Indies," i ngā taukumekume i ngā waenganui i ngā kairaupatu o Pāniora me ngā iwi taketake.
reference: In the 16th century Paraguay, formerly called "The Giant Province of the Indies", was born as a result of the encounter of Spanish conquerors with the native indigenous groups.

source: I tīmata te iwi Pāniora ki ngā mahi raupatu, toru rautau te roa.
reference: The Spaniards started the colonization period which lasted for three centuries.

source: Mai i te orokohanganga mai o Asunciōn i te 1537, kia kaha a Paraguay ki te mau tonu ki te nuinga o ngā tāngata whenua me tōna tuakiri.
reference: Since the foundation of Asunción in 1537, Paraguay has managed to keep a lot of its indigenous character and identity.

source: E mōhio whānuitia nei a Argentina mō tētahi o ngā tīma me ngā kaitākaro papai rawa o te ao mō te polo.
reference: Argentina is well known for having one of the best polo teams and players in the world.

source: Ko te whakataetae nui rawa o te tau ka tū i te marama o Tīhema i ngā papa polo i Las Nanitas.
reference: The largest tournament of the year takes place in December at the polo fields in Las Cañitas.

source: Ka kitea hoki ngā taiwhanga iti me ngā whakataetae ki konei i ētahi atu wāhanga o te tau.
reference: Smaller tournaments and matches can also be seen here at other times of the year.

source: Mō ngā pūrongo e pā ana ki ngā tauwhāinga me ngā wāhi hoko tikiti mō ngā kēmu pōro, tirohia Asociacion Argentina de Polo.
reference: For news on tournaments and where to buy tickets for polo matches, check Asociacion Argentina de Polo.

source: Ko te pūtea tūturu ki te whēnua Falklands ko pāuna Faulkland (FKP), ko tōna wāriu e taurite ana ki te tāra o Ingarangi (GBP).
reference: The official Falklands currency is the Falkland pound (FKP) whose value is set equivalent to that of one British pound (GBP).

source: Ka taea te whakawhiti i ngā moni ki ngā moutere, kei Stanley, kei tua mai i te toa FIC West.
reference: Money can be exchanged at the only bank in the islands which is located in Stanley across from the FIC West store.

source: Ka whakaaetia whānuitia ngā pauna Peretānia puta noa i ngā motu, ā, ki roto tonu i Stanley ka whakaaetia nuitia ngā kāri nama me ngā tāra o Ngā Whenua Tōpū o Amerika.
reference: British pounds will generally be accepted anywhere in the islands and within Stanley credit cards and United States dollars are also often accepted.

source: Ki ngā motu matara kāore pea ngā kāri nama e whakaaetia, ahakoa ka pai pea ngā moni o Peretānia, o Ngā Whenua Tōpū o Amerika hoki; pātai atu ki ngā kaipupuri i mua atu hei whakatau he aha te tikanga utu e whakaaetia ana.
reference: On the outlying islands credit cards will probably not be accepted, although British and United States currency may be taken; check with the owners in advance to determine what is an acceptable payment method.

source: Kāore nei tonu e taea te whakawhiti i te pūtea Falklands ki waho atu o te moutere, nā reira me whakawhiti koe i ō moni i mua i tō wehenga i ngā moutere.
reference: It is nearly impossible to exchange Falklands currency outside of the islands, so exchange money prior to leaving the islands.

source: I te mea kei te tonga a Montevideo i te weheruatanga o te ao, i ngā wā e raumati ana ki reira, e takurua ana ki te Raki, ā huri kau ake hoki.
reference: Since Montevideo is south of the Equator, it is summer there when it's winter in the Northern Hemisphere and vice versa.

source: Kei ngā takiwā pārū a Montevideo; i ngā marama o te raumati ka eke noa ake i te +30 tākiri C te pāmahana.
reference: Montevideo is in the subtropics; in the summer months, temperatures above +30°C are common.

source: He māminga pea te makariri o te hōtoke: kāore ngā paemahana i te tino heke iho ki raro i te kore, engari ka pāhekoheko te hau me te mākū kia makariri iho te rongo i tā te ine mahana.
reference: The winter can be deceptively chilly: temperatures rarely go below freezing, but the wind and humidity combine to make it feel colder than what the thermometer says.

source: Kāore he wā ua, kāore he wā maroke: i te roanga o te tau ka pērā tonu te nui o te ua.
reference: There are no particular "rainy" and "dry" seasons: the amount of rain stays roughly the same throughout the year.

source: He ahakoa e waia ana ngā tini kararehe ki te kite atu i te tangata, he niwhaniwha tonu ngā karerehe , ā, me kauaka e whāngai e whakahōhā rānei.
reference: Though many of the animals in the park are used to seeing humans, the wildlife is nonetheless wild and should not be fed or disturbed.

source: E ai ki ngā mana o te pāka, me noho tonu te 100 iari/mita atu i ngā pea me ngā wuruhi me te 25 iari/mita atu i ērā atu kararehe mohoao katoa!
reference: According to park authorities, stay at least 100 yards/meters away from bears and wolves and 25 yards/meters from all other wild animals!

source: he ahakoa pēhea nei te kōtungatunga o te āhua, e āhei ana te pūrununui, te heka, te Tianui, te pea te āki.
reference: No matter how docile they may look, bison, elk, moose, bears, and nearly all large animals can attack.

source: tau atu tau mai ka aituangia ngā tūruhi i te mea kāore i tika te tawhititanga. He nunui ēnei kararehe, he pāwhara, he mōrearea hoki pea, nāreira hoatu he maheatanga ki a rātou.
reference: Each year, dozens of visitors are injured because they didn't keep a proper distance. These animals are large, wild, and potentially dangerous, so give them their space.

source: Hei āpiti, kia kamakama ka hau mai ngā pea me ngā atu kararehe puihi i te mea o ngā kakara, nā me ngana kia kauka e kawe e tunu rānei i ngā kai kakara, ā, me mā hoki te puni.
reference: In addition, be aware that odors attract bears and other wildlife, so avoid carrying or cooking odorous foods and keep a clean camp.

source: Ko Apia te tāone matua o Hāmoa. Kei te motu o Upolu te tāone, ā, he rahinga tāngata paku iti iho i te 40,000.
reference: Apia is the capital of Samoa. The town is on the island of Upolu and has a population of just under 40,000.

source: I whakatūria a Apia i ngā tau 1850,, ā, koirā te tāone matua ōkawa o Hāmoa mai i te tau 1959.
reference: Apia was founded in the 1850s and has been the official capital of Samoa since 1959.

source: He wāhi mōiriiri te tauranga nei o tētahi pakanga tauā moana e mōhiotia ana i te kino i te tau 1889 i te wā i whanakanau ngā kaipuke e whitu mai i Tiamani, i Ngā Whenua Tōpū o Amerika, i Peretānia anō hoki ki te wehe atu i te tauranga.
reference: The harbor was the site of an infamous naval standoff in 1889 when seven ships from Germany, the US, and Britain refused to leave the harbor.

source: I te totohu o ngā kaipuke katoa, atu i te kaipuke hāereere kotahi nō Peretānia. Tata ki te 200 ngā tāngata no Amerika, nō Tiamani hoki i mate.
reference: All the ships were sunk, except for one British cruiser. Nearly 200 American and German lives were lost.

source: I te wā o te uauatanga mō te tīno rangatiratanga te take, ko te rōpū Mau te rōpū whakahaere. I roto i te huinga mō te rongomau ka mate te ariki a Tupua Tamasese Lealofi te tuatoru.
reference: During the struggle for independence organised by the Mau movement, a peaceful gathering in the town resulted in the killing of the paramount chief Tupua Tamasese Lealofi III.

source: He maha ngā tuaone, i runga i te noho waenga a Tāmaki i ngā whanga e rua. Ko ngā mea rorotu kei ngā wāhi e toru.
reference: There are many beaches, due to Auckland's straddling of two harbours. The most popular ones are in three areas.

source: Ko ngā takutai ki Te Raki Paewhenua (kei Te Whanganui a Toi) kei Te Moana nui a Kiwa, e toro ana mai i Long Bay ki Devonport.
reference: North Shore beaches (in North Harbour district) are on the Pacific Ocean and stretch from Long Bay in the north to Devonport in the south.

source: Ko te nuinga o ngā moana haumaru nei, ā, ko te nuinga hoki kua whakamarua e te rākau pōhutukawa.
reference: They are almost all sandy beaches with safe swimming, and most have shade provided by pohutukawa trees.

source: Kei te Pūahatanga Waitemata ngā taha moana o Tamaki Drive, ki ngā wāhi tāone whai rawa o Mission Bay me St Heliers ki te pito o Tāmaki Makaurau.
reference: Tamaki Drive beaches are on the Waitemata Harbour, in the upmarket suburbs of Mission Bay and St Heliers in Central Auckland.

source: I ētahi wā he wāhi i kī ana i ngā whānau tēnei moana me ngā tini toa papai e tata ana ki te tai. He kaukau haumaru.
reference: These are sometimes-crowded family beaches with a good range of shops lining the shore. Swimming is safe.

source: Ko te pia ā-rohe matua ko te "Nama tahi", ehara i te pia uaua, ēngari he māma he whakarekareka. Ko te atu pia ā-rohe ko te "Manta"
reference: The main local beer is 'Number One', it is not a complex beer, but pleasant and refreshing. The other local beer is called "Manta".

source: He nui ngā waina Wīwī, engari ka whāroa ake pea te totoro atu o ngā waina a Aotearoa me Ahitereiria.
reference: There are many French wines to be had, but the New Zealand and Australian wines might travel better.

source: He pai noa iho te inu wai mai i ngā kōrere ki reira, heoi mēnā e wehi ana, he māmā te kite i ngā wai pātara.
reference: The local tap water is perfectly safe to drink, but bottled water is easy to find if you are fearful.

source: Mo ngā Ahitereiria, ko te ariā mō te kawhe "papatahi mā' he manene. Ko te short black he "espresso", ko te cappuccino he kirimi kei te tihi (ehara i te whorowho), ā, karekau te tī kua ranu me te miraka.
reference: For Australians, the idea of 'flat white' coffee is foreign. A short black is 'espresso', cappuccino comes heaped high with cream (not froth), and tea is served without milk.

source: He pēnei i te taumata o Belgian te tiakarete wera nei. He nui te utu mō ngā inu huarākau ēngari he tīno reka.
reference: The hot chocolate is up to Belgian standards. Fruit juices are pricey but excellent.

source: He maha ngā haerenga ki te ākau puta noa i te tau, ā, kāore e kitea whānuitia ana ngā whara i runga i tētahi o ēnei take ki te ākau.
reference: Many trips to the reef are made all year around, and injuries due to any of these causes on the reef are rare.

source: Engari tonu, whāia ngā tohutohu a ngā rangatita, me aro hoki ki ngā tohu haumaru.
reference: Still, take advice from authorities, obey all signs, and pay close attention to safety warnings.

source: Ka kitea ngā momo Tīwai nei ki tātahi me te taha o ngā awa mai i te marama o Oketopa me Aperira, ki te raki o 1700. I ētahi ka kitea i waho atu i ēnei wā.
reference: Box jellyfish occur near beaches and near river estuaries from October to April north of 1770. They can occasionally be found outside these times.

source: He mea tūturu ngā mangō, heoi anō he iti rawa tā rātou whakaeke i te tangata. Kei te mataku te nuinga o ngā mangō i te tangata, ka kaukau noa atu.
reference: Sharks do exist, however they rarely attack humans. Most sharks are scared of humans and would swim away.

source: Kāore ngā waitā korokotaira e tīno noho ki rō moana, ko tō rātou tīno wāhi noho ko ngā awaawa kei te raki o Rockhampton.
reference: Saltwater Crocodiles do not actively live in the ocean, their primary habitat is in river estuaries north from Rockhampton.

source: Mā te whakarite i mua e tau ai te hinengaro a te tangata hāereere he wāhi māna hei moe ina tae atu ki te wāhi rā.
reference: Booking in advance gives the traveller peace of mind that they will have somewhere to sleep once they arrive at their destination.

source: He nui ngā wā he whakaritenga ā ngā kaiwhakarite haerenga ki ētahi hōtēra, ahakoa ka taea pea e koe te whakarite i ētahi momo noho, pērā i ngā puninga, mā tētahi kaiwhakarite haerenga.
reference: Travel agents often have deals with specific hotels, although you may find it possible to book other forms of accommodation, like camping grounds, through a travel agent.

source: He rite tonu te tuku a ngā kaiwhakarite hāereere i ngā kete kei roto rā ko te parakuihi, ngā whakaritenga ikiiki ki/atu i te tauranga wakarererangi, ngā kete paheko i te rerenga me te hōtēra rānei.
reference: Travel agents usually offer packages that include breakfast, transportation arrangements to/from the airport or even combined flight and hotel packages.

source: Ka taea hoki e rātou te pupuri i te whakaritenga māu ki te hiahia wā koe hei whakaaro ki te tukunga, ki te whiwhi rānei i ētahi atu puka mō te wāhi ka torohia e koe (hei tauira, he pane uruuruwhenua).
reference: They can also hold the reservation for you if you need time to think about the offer or procure other documents for your destination (e.g. visa).

source: Tukuna ngā whakatikatika me ngā tono ki te kaitiaki haereere i te tuatahi, kaua ko te hōtēra.
reference: Any amendments or requests though should be coursed through the travel agent first and not directly with the hotel.

source: Mō ētahi taiopenga, ko te nuinga o ngā kaitautoko o ngā huihuinga waiata nei ka hiahia ki te whakapuni ki rō papa, ā, ko te nuinga o ngā kaitautoko ka whakatau koinaka tētahi o ngā tīno wāhanga o te wheakoranga.
reference: For some festivals, the vast majority of the attendants to music festivals decide to camp on site, and most attendants consider it a vital part of the experience.

source: Me kei te hiahia koe kia tata atu ki ngā mahi, me tae wawe atu koe kia whai wāhi atu ki tētahi puni tata ki ngā waiata.
reference: If you want to be close to the action you're going to have to get in early to get a camping site close to the music.

source: Me maumahara ahakoa kua mutu pea te puoro ki ngā atamira matua, tērā ētahi wāhi o te hui ahurei e whakatangi puoro tai nuaku ana kia waenganui pō.
reference: Remember that even though music on the main stages may have finished, there may be sections of the festival that will keep playing music until late into the night.

source: Kei ētahi hui ahurei he wāhi tēneti motuhake mā ngā whānau whai tamariki nohinohi.
reference: Some festivals have special camping areas for families with young children.

source: Mēnā e whakawhiti ana koe i te awa ki Northen Baltic, tirohia ngā wāhi hōpuni i te mea ko te haere i roto i te huka he hoihoi mō te hunga ka pāngia e tērā momo mate.
reference: If crossing the Northern Baltic in winter, check the cabin location, as going through ice causes quite horrible noise for those most affected.

source: Kei roto i ngā rerenga Saint Petersburg he wā ki rō tāone. Kei te wātea ngā pāhihi rerenga i ngā herenga pane uruwhenua (tirohia ngā herenga).
reference: Saint Petersburg cruises include time in town. Cruise passengers are exempted from visa requirements (check the terms).

source: I ētahi wā ka whakapau kaha ngā whare petipeti kia nui ake ai te wā me te moni e whakapaungia ana e ngā manuhiri. I te nuinga o te wā kāore i te kitea ngā wini, ngā karaka rānei, ā, he uaua te kitea o ngā putanga.
reference: Casinos typically make many efforts to maximize time and money spent by guests. Windows and clocks are usually absent, and exits can be hard to find.

source: I te nuinga o te wā he ratonga kai, inu, whakangahau motuhake, kia noho hari tonu ngā manuhiri, kia noho tonu rātou ki te whare.
reference: They usually have special food, drink and entertainment offers, to keep guests in a good mood, and keep them at the premise.

source: Tērā ētahi hingonga ka koha atu i te inu waipiro. Heoi, ka riro te tangata i roto i tana haurangi, kei te mōhio hoki ngā kaipetipeti toa ki te hirahiratanga o te noho haurangi kore.
reference: Some venues offer alcoholic beverages on the house. However, drunkenness impairs judgement, and all good gamblers know the importance of staying sober.

source: Me whai whakaaro te tangata ka taraiwa ake ki ngā ahopae teitei, ki ngā tarahanga maunga rānei kei pā pea te hukarere, te tio, ngā paemahana tino makariri rānei.
reference: Anyone who's going to drive at high latitudes or over mountain passes should consider the possibility of snow, ice, or freezing temperatures.

source: Ki ngā huarahi tio, hukapapa hoki, he iti te pane uruwhenua, ā, e kore e taea e koe te taraiwa anō nei kei te raima kau koe.
reference: On icy and snowy roadways, friction is low and you cannot drive as if you were on bare asphalt.

source: I ngā huka kairākau, ka tau pea te nui o ngā huka kia mau koe i te wā poto rawa.
reference: During blizzards, enough snow to get you stuck can fall in very little time.

source: Ka whakawhāititia hoki pea te ari e te huka rere, te huka pupuhi rānei, e te tōtā, te tio rānei ki ngā matapihi o te waka.
reference: Visibility may also be restricted by falling or blowing snow or by condensation or ice on vehicle windows.

source: Ki tērā atu taha, he noa ngā āhuatanga tio, hukapapa hoki ki ngā tini whenua, ā, ka haere tonu te ikiiki kāore he tino raru puta noa i te tau.
reference: On the other hand, icy and snowy conditions are normal in many countries, and traffic goes on mostly uninterrupted all year round.

source: Ākuanei pea ko ngā Safari ngā tino mahinga tūruhi ki Awherika me te tīno mahinga mō te tini o ngā tūruhi.
reference: Safaris are perhaps the greatest tourism draw in Africa and the highlight for many visitors.

source: Ko te kupu safari e whakamahi whanuingia ana hei āhuatanga haerere ki uta ki tai ki te titiro ki ngā kararehe mohoao o Awherika, inā koā ki te wāhanga Savanna.
reference: The term safari in popular use refers to overland travel to view the stunning African wildlife, particularly on savanna.

source: Ētahi kararehe pēnei i ngā arewhana me ngā Hirāwhe ka whakatata atu i ētahi wā ki ngā motokā, ā, mā te taputapu ngāwari noa e whai wāhi atu ai ki te tirohanga papai.
reference: Some animals, such as elephants and giraffes, tend to approach closely to cars and standard equipment will allow good viewing.

source: Ka whakamā i ētahi wā ngā raiona, ngā tīta, ngā rēpara hoki, ā, ka pai ake tō kite i a rātou ki ngā karu whakatata.
reference: Lions, cheetahs and leopards are sometimes shy and you will see them better with binoculars.

source: Ko tētahi safari hīkoi (e kīia ana hoki tētahi "hīkoi ngahere", "safari takahi whenua", te haere "mā raro" rānei) ko te hīkoi, mō ētahi hāora, ētahi rangi rānei.
reference: A walking safari (also called a "bush walk", "hiking safari", or going "footing") consists of hiking, either for a few hours or several days.

source: Ka tū Ngā Taumāhekeheke o te Ao mō te Hunga Pararūtiki atu i te 24 o Ākuhata ki te 5 o Hepetema hei te tau 2021. Ka tū ētahi o ngā mahi ki wāhi kē atu puta noa i Hapani.
reference: The Paralympics will take place from 24 August to 5 September 2021. Some events will be held in other locations throughout Japan.

source: Ka tū a Tokyo hei tāone nui kotahi nō Āhia kia manaaki i ngā Taumāhekeheke o te Ao ā-raumati e rua, i runga i tana manaaki i ngā taumāhekeheke i te tau 1964.
reference: Tokyo will be the only Asian city to have hosted two summer Olympics, having hosted the games in 1964.

source: Mēnā i hoko koe i ō rerenga, i tō whare noho mō te tau 2020 i mua i te whakapuakitanga o te takaroatanga, ka raru pea.
reference: If you booked your flights and accommodation for 2020 before the postponement was announced, you may have a tricky situation.

source: Ka rerekē ngā kaupapa here whakakore, ēngari i te mutunga o te Māihe ko te nuinga o ngā kaupapa here whakakorenga huaketo kāore i toro ki te Hurae 2020, i te wā i whakaritea mai ai ngā kēmu taumāhekeheke o te ao.
reference: Cancellation policies vary, but as of late March most coronavirus-based cancellation policies don't extend to July 2020, when the Olympics had been scheduled.

source: Ka whakaarotia ko te utu o te nuinga o ngā tīkiti whakaaturanga kei waenganui i te 2,500 yen me te 130,000 yen, ā, ko ngā tīkiti whānui he tata ki te 7,000 yen.
reference: It's expected that most event tickets will cost between ¥2,500 and ¥130,000, with typical tickets costing around ¥7,000.

source: Mā te haeana kākahu mākū e āwhina pea te whakamaroke. He maha ngā hōtēra whai haeana me te papa haeana e wātea ana ki te whakamahi, ahakoa kāore tētahi ki te ruma.
reference: Ironing damp clothes can help them dry. Many hotels have an iron and ironing board available for loan, even if one is not present in the room.

source: Ki te kore he aiana, ki te kore rānei koe e pai ki te mau i ngā tōkena iana, tēnā, e taea nei te whakamahi i tētahi mihini whakamaroke makawe mēnā kei reira tētahi.
reference: If an iron isn't available, or if you don't fancy wearing ironed socks, then you can try using a hairdryer, if available.

source: Kia tūpato kia kaua e tuku i te kākahu kia wera rawa (mā korā pea e pīngongo, i ētahi wā kino ka hunuhunutia rānei).
reference: Be careful not to allow fabric to become too hot (which can cause shrinkage, or in extreme cases, scorch).

source: He ara rerekē hei whakamā i te wai, ka pai ake ētahi ki ētahi whakatuma ake.
reference: There are different ways of purifying water, some more effective against specific threats.

source: I ētahi wāhi he nawhe te koropupū i te wai mō te kotahi miniti, i ētahi atu wāhi me nui atu ngā miniti.
reference: In some areas boiling water for a minute is enough, in others several minutes are needed.

source: Ka rerekē te painga o ngā tātari, ā, mehemea he āwangawanga tāu, me whai whakaaro koe ki te hoko i tō wai ki tētahi pātara kua kōpanitia mai i tētahi kamupene e mōhiotia ana.
reference: Filters vary in effectiveness, and should you have a concern, then you should consider buying your water in a sealed bottle from a reputable company.

source: Ka kite pea te hunga hāereere i ngā riha kararehe kāore i te waia ki ēnei ki ō rātou wā kāinga.
reference: Travellers may encounter animal pests that they are not familiar with in their home regions.

source: Ka taea e ngā riha te whakapirau kai, te whakaputa hīrawerawe, ā, ko te mea kino rawa ka whakaputa mate tāwera, te whakawhānui paitini, te tuku poke rānei.
reference: Pests can spoil food, cause irritation, or in a worse case cause allergic reactions, spread venom, or transmit infections.

source: Ko ngā mate hōrapa ake, ko ngā kararehe mōrearea rānei ka taea te whara, te whakamate rānei i te tangata mā te tōpana, i te nuinga o te wā kāore i te kīia he kīrearea.
reference: Infectious diseases themselves, or dangerous animals that can injure or kill people by force, do not usually qualify as pests.

source: Ko te hokohoko tāke kore he ara hei hoko i ngā rawa karekau he here ki te tāka e mahia ana ki ētahi wāhi motuhake.
reference: Duty free shopping is the opportunity to buy goods exempted from taxes and excises at certain locations.

source: I ētahi wā e āhei ai te hunga hāereere ki ngā motu he nui te tāke kia whakaemi i ētahi moni nui, ā, e pēnei ana hoki mō ngā taputapu pēnei i ngā inu waipiro me ngā tupeka anō hoki.
reference: Travellers bound for countries with heavy taxation can sometimes save a considerable amount of money, especially on products such as alcoholic beverages and tobacco.

source: Ko te wāhanga i waenganui i Point Marion me Fairmont kei te whakatū i ngā āhuatanga taraiwa uaua rawa ki te Buffalo-Pittsburgh Highway, he rite tonu te haere mā ngā whenua wao mohoao.
reference: The stretch between Point Marion and Fairmont presents the most challenging driving conditions on the Buffalo-Pittsburgh Highway, passing frequently through isolated backwoods terrain.

source: Ki te kore koe e waia ana ki te taraiwa ki ngā huarahi taiwhenua, kia mataara tonu koe: he nui ngā paripari, ngā huarahi whāiti, me ngā kokonga koki tāhapa.
reference: If you're not used to driving on country roads, keep your wits about you: steep grades, narrow lanes, and sharp curves predominate.

source: He iti iho ngā here tere e tohua ana i ērā ki ngā wāhanga o mua, o muri anō hoki - he nui te wā ko te 35-40 m/h (56-64 km/h), - ā, he mea hira rawa te tino whai i ēnei i ērā atu.
reference: Posted speed limits are noticeably lower than in previous and subsequent sections — commonly 35-40 mph (56-64 km/h) — and strict obedience to them is even more important than otherwise.

source: He mea māhirahira, he kaha ake te ratonga waea pūkoro ki konei i ērā atu roanga o te huarahi, hei tauira ngā Pennsylvania Wilds.
reference: Curiously, though, mobile phone service is much stronger here than along many other stretches of the route, e.g. the Pennsylvania Wilds.

source: He āhua reka ngā parāoa Tiamani, ā, ki Bavaria, he āhua mōmona, he nui ngā momo, pērā i ērā o tā rātou whenua tata ki te tonga, o Ateria.
reference: German pastries are quite good, and in Bavaria, are quite rich and varied, similar to those of their southern neighbor, Austria.

source: E kitea noatia ana ngā tāte huarākau, arā ka tunua ngā āporo ki ngā tāte puta noa i te tau, ā, ka kitea ngā tiere, ngā paramu i te wā o te raumati.
reference: Fruit pastries are common, with apples cooked into pastries year round, and cherries and plums making their appearances during the summer.

source: Kei ngā tini tunumanga parāoa Tiamani he aramona, he hānati, he nati rākau atu anō hoki. He tino pai te kai tahi i ngā keke rorotu ki tētahi kapu kawhe hūnene.
reference: Many German baked goods also feature almonds, hazelnuts, and other tree nuts. Popular cakes often pair particularly well with a cup of strong coffee.

source: Me kei te hiahia koe i ētahi paoa rekareka iti nei, taraingia te aha e ai ki te rohe kua ingoa Berliner, Pfannkuchen, Krapfen rānei.
reference: If you want some small though rich pastries, try what depending on region are called Berliner, Pfannkuchen or Krapfen.

source: Ko te kai kari tētahi kai kua whakaritea ki ngā otaota, ki ngā rau kikini, tahi ki te mīti, te huawhenua rānei.
reference: A curry is a dish based on herbs and spices, together with either meat or vegetables.

source: He "maroke", he "mākū" rānei tētahi kari i runga i te nui o te wē.
reference: A curry can be either "dry" or "wet" depending on the amount of liquid.

source: Ki ngā rohe tuawhenua o Īnia ki te Raki, o Pakitāne, kei te whakamahia whānuitia te miraka tepe ki ngā kari; ki Īnia ki te Tonga, ki ētahi atu takiwā ki tai o te paparahi āpiti, kei te whakamahia whānuitia te miraka kokonati.
reference: In inland regions of Northern India and Pakistan, yogurt is commonly used in curries; in Southern India and some other coastal regions of the subcontinent, coconut milk is commonly used.

source: I roto i te 17,000 ngā motu hei kōwhiringa, ko te kai Indonesia hei āhuatanga hamarara e whakakapi nei i te tini i ngā kai taketake ā-rohe e horapa ana i te whenua.
reference: With 17,000 islands to choose from, Indonesian food is an umbrella term covering a vast variety of regional cuisines found across the nation.

source: Heoi anō, mehemea ka whakamahia karekau ngā kupu anō, ko te tino tikanga o te kupu ko te kai i ahu mai i ngā wāhi waenga, tawhiti hoki o te motu matua o Java.
reference: But, if used without further qualifiers, the term tends to mean the food originally from the central and eastern parts of the main island Java.

source: Ināianei kei te wātea mai ngā momo kai Javanese puta noa i te huinga motu, ā, kei a ia te whānuitanga o ngā kai he māmā noa te hā, ā, ko ngā hā matua he pai ki te hunga Javanese ko te pīnati, te hiri, te huka (inarā ko te huka kokonati Javanese), me ngā tini rau kikini whakakakara.
reference: Now widely available throughout the archipelago, Javanese cuisine features an array of simply seasoned dishes, the predominant flavorings the Javanese favor being peanuts, chillies, sugar (especially Javanese coconut sugar) and various aromatic spices.

source: He mea tautoko ngā tarapu mō ngā waewae o te kaieke ka tau ki raro ki ngā taha e rua o te tera.
reference: Stirrups are supports for the rider's feet that hang down on either side of the saddle.

source: Ka hoatu rātou he tiakitanga pai ake mō te kaieke ēngari he āwangawangatanga tonu mō te haumarutanga i te mea o ngā waewae o te kaieke kei mau.
reference: They provide greater stability for the rider but can have safety concerns due to the potential for a rider's feet to get stuck in them.

source: Ki te whiua tētahi kaieke i tētahi hōiho engari kua mau te waewae ki te tarapu, ka tōngia pea mēnā ka oma ake te hōiho. Hei whakaiti rawa i tēnei mōrearea, ka taea ētahi tūpatotanga haumaru.
reference: If a rider is thrown from a horse but has a foot caught in the stirrup, they could be dragged if the horse runs away. To minimize this risk, a number of safety precautions can be taken.

source: Mātua, ko te nuinga o ngā kaieke ka mau pūtu tika pōtae tika me ngā mau rekereke tika, me te kuiti māeneene whāiti nei.
reference: First, most riders wear riding boots with a heel and a smooth, quite narrow, sole.

source: E whai ake nei ko ētahi terapēke, inarā ko ngā terapēke Ingarihi, he pā marutau ōna e āhei ai te tarapu kirikau te taka i te terapēke mēnā kua tōia whakamuritia e tētahi kai eke e taka ana.
reference: Next, some saddles, particularly English saddles, have safety bars that allow a stirrup leather to fall off the saddle if pulled backwards by a falling rider.

source: Te Taiororua o Cochamō - Te wāhi whakaeke kounga rawa o Hiri, e mōhiotia ana ko te Yosemite o Amerika ki te Tonga, me ana momo pakitara me ngā kōhatu tokapata nunui.
reference: Cochamó Valley - Chile's premier climbing destination, known as the Yosemite of South America, with a variety of granite big walls and crags.

source: Kei roto i te piki ki ngā tihi ko ngā tirohanga mīharo i ngā tihi. Kei te whakatūtū tonu ngā kaipiki mai i ngā tōpito katoa o te ao i ngā ara hou ki ōna pakitara mutunga kore.
reference: Summits include breath-taking views from peaks. Climbers from all parts of the world are continually establishing new routes amongst its endless potential of walls.

source: Ko ngā hākinakina heke haere, kei roto rā ko te retireti me te eke papa hukapapa, he hākinakina makau ko te mahi ko te rere iho i te whenua e kapia ana ki te hukapapa ki ngā retireti, ki tētahi papa hukapapa rānei e tūhonotia ana ki ō waeawae.
reference: Downhill snowsports, which include skiing and snowboarding, are popular sports involving sliding down snow-covered terrain with skis or a snowboard attached to your feet.

source: He hanga haerere te retireti me ngā tini tāngata hikaka, e mōhiotia whānuitia ki te "reti nono", te whakarite i ngā hararei reti i tētahi wāhi motuhake.
reference: Skiing is a major travelling activity with many enthusiasts, occasionally known as "ski bums," planning entire vacations around skiing at a particular location.

source: He tawhito rawa te ariā o te eke rēti - kei te kitea he toi ana e whakaatu ana i ngā kaieke rēti mai rā anō i te 5000 BC!
reference: The idea of skiing is very old — cave paintings depicting skiers date back as far as 5000 BC!

source: Mai i te rautau tua 17, neke atu rānei, te whakaretireti whakaheke hei hākinakina, ā, i te tau 1861 i whakatuwheratia te karapu retireti tuatahi e ētahi tāngata nō Nōwei ki Ahitereiria.
reference: Downhill skiing as a sport goes back to at least the 17th century, and in 1861 the first recreational ski club was opened by Norwegians in Australia.

source: Paketeke mā te retihuka: ko tēnei mahinga e kī ana hoki ko te reti reti i ngā whenua o muri, ko te reti tūruhi, ko te reti hīkoinga rānei.
reference: Backpacking by ski: This activity is also called backcountry ski, ski touring or ski hiking.

source: E hāngai ana ki te haere retireti āhua maunga, te piki maunga rānei, engari i te nuinga o te wā kāore i te whai wāhi ēnei, ko ēnei e mahia ana ki ngā wāhi paripari, ā, ka hiahiatia ngā reti me ngā pūtu māro ake.
reference: It is related to but usually not involving alpine style ski touring or mountaineering, the latter ones done in steep terrain and requiring much stiffer skis and boots.

source: Whakaarohia te ara retireti hei ara hīkoi ōrite.
reference: Think of the skiing route as of a similar hiking route.

source: I roto i ngā āhuatanga papai ka roa ake ngā roanga ka taea e koe i tā te hīkoi - heoi i ētahi wā iti nei ka tae rawa koe ki te terenga o te retireti hīkoi hukapapa mēnā horekau tētahi pīkau taumaha ki ngā ara kua whakaritea.
reference: In good conditions you will be able to cover somewhat greater distances than walking – but only very seldom you will get the speeds of cross country skiing without a heavy backpack in groomed tracks.

source: He iti noa nei te whenua whānui a Ūropi engari he nui ngā iwi motuhake kei reira. Ko tōna āhua, ko te karore i ngā whenua huhua he nui ngā wā me whakamātauria ngā pane uruwhenua e uru ai ki aua whenua rā.
reference: Europe is a continent that is relatively small but with many independent countries. Under normal circumstances, travelling through multiple countries would mean having to go through visa applications and passport control multiple times.

source: Heoi anō, he āhua pērā i tētahi whenua te Schengen zone, mō tēnei āhuatanga.
reference: The Schengen zone, however, works somewhat like one country in this respect.

source: I a koe ka noho ki tēnei rohe, ka taea e koe te whakawhiti i ngā rohe, ā, ehara i te mea me haere anō mā ngā tirotiro kaitiaki uruwhenua.
reference: As long as you stay in this zone, you can generally cross borders without going through passport control checkpoints again.

source: Me te mea hoki, mā te mau i tētahi visa Schengen me tono motuhake koe mō ngā visas kei ia o ngā whenua mema Schengen, ā, koira e maha ake te wā, te moni me ngā pepamahi.
reference: Similarly, by having a Schengen visa, you do not need to apply for visas to each of the Schengen member countries separately, hence saving time, money and paperwork.

source: Kāore he mōhiotanga whānui hei tautohu i ngā taputapu hei taputapu nō tua whakarere. Ētahi kaiwhakahaere tāki ka tautohu i ngā taputapu nō tua whakarere mēnā e nui atu i te 100 tau te tawhito.
reference: There is no universal definition for which manufactured items are antiques. Some tax agencies define goods older than 100 years as antiques.

source: He rerekē ā-matawhenua nei ngā whakamāramatanga, ka rerekē pea te tau ngā tau kei ngā whenua, pērā ki Te Raki o Amerika me Ūropi.
reference: The definition has geographic variations, where the age limit might be shorter in places such as North America than in Europe.

source: Ko ngā rawa mahi-a-ringa ka kīa he tāonga nō uki, he ahakoa i hangaia i muri i ētahi tāonga kua whakaputaina nuitia.
reference: Handicraft products might be defined as antiques, though they are younger than similar mass-produced goods.

source: He hirahira te pāmu renetia mō te oranga o te Sāmi, ā, ko te ahurea e hāngai ana ki te hohoko he hirahira ki ngā umanga maha.
reference: Reindeer husbandry is an important livelihood among the Sámi and the culture surrounding the trade is important also for many with other professions.

source: Ahakoa i ngā wā o mua, kāore ngā Sāmi katoa i whai wāhi ki te mahi pāmu nui i ngā renetia, ēngari i ora nā te hī ika, nā te aru, nā ngā mahi pēnei, ko te renetia i whakamahia hei kararehe mahi i te nuinga o te wā.
